1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d7ed9fe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F74%2F6f%2F9b6933b24bdd9d8add02dd41b2af%2Fbookstore-2603130118.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e3cf2fe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F74%2F6f%2F9b6933b24bdd9d8add02dd41b2af%2Fbookstore-2603130118.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="BOOKSTORE_2603130118.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e3cf2fe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F74%2F6f%2F9b6933b24bdd9d8add02dd41b2af%2Fbookstore-2603130118.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/13f9dee/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F74%2F6f%2F9b6933b24bdd9d8add02dd41b2af%2Fbookstore-2603130118.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e3cf2fe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F74%2F6f%2F9b6933b24bdd9d8add02dd41b2af%2Fbookstore-2603130118.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Throughout the store, book lovers will find stickers, postcards, candles and notebooks tailored to their interests. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Destination for the neighborhood</h3><p>Since opening, the 1,500-square-foot space has been a destination for families. A corner of Partners in Crime is dedicated to children’s books.</p><p>“It was important for us that we were still a bookstore for the neighborhood,” Jeff said. “Because, not everyone reads romance, not everyone reads mystery, but we hope … since we've got true crime, nonfiction, a young adult section, a kids' section. … There’s something for everyone.”</p><p>Amanda was excited to platform many indie writers — self-published and from smaller publishers — who are often overlooked.</p><p>“I gravitated toward a lot of the indies and the smaller authors, just because there are a lot of people that aren't out there, that we're not hearing their voices,” she said. “There are so many underrepresented Black authors and things like that. And I'm just like, we want your voice. We want to hear you.”</p><p>And in Partners in Crime, the two genres are broken down even further into subgenres, such as romantic suspense, cozy mystery and romantic fantasy, making it easy for readers to find what they're looking for.</p><p>"The big bookstore chain... [is] just focusing on stuff that sells really well, which then ultimately, I think, hurts readers, because they're not going to bump into a lot of books that are mid-lists,” Amanda said.</p><p>So far, business has been positive and encouraging, Jeff said. A book club is planned, as well as other community events.</p><p>"I'm just happy it's doing well enough that I'm like, OK, we can stay open... we can pay our bills,” he said.</p><p>Throughout the store are stickers, postcards, candles and notebooks tailored to book lovers. Seating in the back was chosen to make all customers feel welcomed.</p><p>"And that's because that's the big thing about romance, is that anybody can see themselves in it,” Amanda said. “And everybody's represented."</p>
entries.author: Mariah Rush

entries.title: Brendan Hunt, co-star of ‘Ted Lasso,’ brings his one-man show to the city that shaped him
entries.summary: <p>Things finally fell into place for Brendan Hunt after a fateful meeting with Paul McCartney.</p><p>This spring, Hunt, a Chicago native best known for his role as Coach Beard on the Apple TV+ series “Ted Lasso,” brings a deeply personal performance to the city where he started.</p><p>The production, titled <a class="Link" href="https://www.steppenwolf.org/tickets--events/seasons-/2025-26/the-movement-you-need/" target="_blank" ><u>“The Movement You Need: An Evening with Brendan Hunt,”</u></a> takes inspiration from a line in the Beatles’ song “Hey Jude.” The 85-minute one-man show runs April 19 through May 10 at the Steppenwolf Theatre; fellow Chicago native <a class="Link" href="https://www.ashleyrodbro.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Ashley Rodbro</u></a> directs.</p><p>Based on his upbringing and his complicated relationship with his late mother, rooted in their shared love of The Beatles, the show is framed by something Hunt had been hoping for his entire life: meeting McCartney, which happened by chance in 2022.</p><p>By that point, the solo show had already begun to take shape. The idea had been percolating in his mind for several years after his mother’s death.Hunt, 53, is an alum of <a class="Link" href="https://www.secondcity.com/" target="_blank" ><u>The Second City</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://boomchicago.nl/" target="_blank" ><u>Boom Chicago</u></a>. He said the initial idea for the show blossomed the moment he became a father. Hunt’s oldest son, born in 2021, entered the world as a Beatles song began playing. “I don’t want to get into [that] too much because that’s a big moment in the show. But after that, I sort of realized The Beatles tend to show up in my life in a bunch of ways,” Hunt said in an interview.</p><p>Everything snapped into focus when Hunt’s longtime friend and colleague Jason Sudeikis invited him to tag along to one of McCartney’s rehearsals. They were already in England, busy filming “Ted Lasso,” when they trekked to Wembley Stadium to hear McCartney prepare for a tribute concert for Taylor Hawkins, the late Foo Fighters drummer.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ba0000" name="image-ba0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c7d0c8b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb8%2F9c%2Fcc449b7e4c5280a2b8aaf7d6ac81%2Fbrendanhunt-260418-16.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d64ca9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb8%2F9c%2Fcc449b7e4c5280a2b8aaf7d6ac81%2Fbrendanhunt-260418-16.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/13b14c4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb8%2F9c%2Fcc449b7e4c5280a2b8aaf7d6ac81%2Fbrendanhunt-260418-16.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="BRENDANHUNT_260418-16.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/13b14c4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb8%2F9c%2Fcc449b7e4c5280a2b8aaf7d6ac81%2Fbrendanhunt-260418-16.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6a448f1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb8%2F9c%2Fcc449b7e4c5280a2b8aaf7d6ac81%2Fbrendanhunt-260418-16.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/13b14c4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb8%2F9c%2Fcc449b7e4c5280a2b8aaf7d6ac81%2Fbrendanhunt-260418-16.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Brendan Hunt says, “I was rooting for the Sox and the Cubs before anyone told me that I wasn’t allowed to root for both.” </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Hunt, not wanting to scare McCartney off by gushing over him like every other starstruck fan, kept most of his thoughts to himself. But the “awkwardness” of that encounter “ended up offering an opportunity to make the show better,” Hunt said. </p><p>So the next summer, he workshopped “The Movement You Need” at the Hollywood Fringe Festival in front of a crowd of about 30 people.“The show itself is so little about meeting Paul McCartney,” Hunt said. “But the setup … and the way it sort of comes back around at the end made the show seem way more finished than it actually was, way earlier than it was,” Hunt said. “After that very first one, I was like, "Oh, oh s— ." It was just immediately different.”</p><p>After the success of the Hollywood Fringe Show, Hunt’s performance made its New York City premiere at the SoHo Playhouse. Now, it’s coming to Steppenwolf, which Hunt says he has admired <a class="Link" href="https://news.illinoisstate.edu/2011/05/steppenwolf-isus-theatre-program-prepared-steppenwolf-founders/" target="_blank" ><u>since his days at Illinois State University</u></a>, where he got a degree in theater.</p><p>Having a one-man show at a venue he’s revered since his college days is “pretty f—ing exciting,” Hunt said.</p><p>“I just have such reverence for that place. I hope that they’re not in such shambles that they would want someone such as me to be on their stage for a while,” he said jokingly.</p><p>Hunt said “The Movement You Need” will have a bit of “Ted Lasso” DNA baked into it. “We’re able to get into sort of emotional stuff, but then — just when you think it’s about to go too far — we pop the balloon and pull you back into comedy,” he said.</p><p>As far as season 4 of “Ted Lasso” goes, filming wrapped up earlier this year, Hunt said. While the actor couldn’t divulge any details, it is known that the highly anticipated new season, expected to hit streaming in August, will feature an all-women’s soccer team.</p><p>“I can only tell you that we had a really, really good time getting back into it,” Hunt said. “We’ll see if it’s any good or not, but we sure like doing it.”Separately, Hunt is working on a memoir that explores life through his eyes as a non-partisan Chicago baseball fan.</p><p>“I was rooting for the Sox and the Cubs before anyone told me that I wasn’t allowed to root for both,” Hunt said.</p><p>For years, he’s hidden his love of both teams like some kind of “horrible secret.” But now, Hunt says, he’s tired of it. “I'm living my truth. I like both teams.”<br></p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: After rocky first year, Chicago’s top federal prosecutor says he isn’t taking orders from Washington
entries.summary: <p>When Chicago’s top federal prosecutor is asked if his office takes marching orders from Washington, he doesn’t mince words.</p><p>“You should write this down,” U.S. Attorney Andrew Boutros recently told a Chicago Sun-Times reporter.</p><p>“[There is] not a single case, ok, involving politics in our decision making. Full stop. Period. Zero,” Boutros said. “And anyone who says otherwise is misstating reality. And anyone who says otherwise is an armchair expert who doesn’t know what they’re talking about. Period.”</p><p>He said he isn’t done with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >Operation Midway Blitz</a>. Nor <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-corruption-trials" target="_blank" >public corruption</a> — he said people should “stay tuned.”<b> </b>And having reached the end of a volatile first year in office, in which President Donald Trump has been accused of weaponizing the Justice Department, Boutros said he has cases to bring “based on facts, law and the equities.”</p><p>But his first year also featured an “unprecedented exodus” of prosecutors from the office he leads, critics note. Most section chiefs left. And convictions have yet to emerge from the flurry of charges the office filed during Midway Blitz, the aggressive deportation campaign that hit the Chicago area last year.</p><p>It charged <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" >Marimar Martinez</a>, the woman shot by a Border Patrol agent and labeled a “domestic terrorist” before<b> </b>the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/20/operation-midway-blitz-marimar-martinez-anthony-ruiz-us-supreme-court-feds-drop-case-against-woman-shot-by-border-patrol-agent-in-brighton-park" target="_blank" >charges were dropped</a>. And<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/11/juan-espinoza-martinez-cleared-of-bovino-murder-plot-still-in-detention-after-judge-denies-bail" target="_blank" >Juan<b> </b>Espinoza<b> </b>Martinez</a>, the man <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/22/federal-jury-rejects-bovino-murder-plot-after-called-upon-stand-up-overreaching-government" target="_blank" >acquitted of putting a hit</a> on then-<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/12/gregory-bovino-border-patrol-deportation-elcentro-california-blowing-rock-boone-north-carolina-migrants-immigration" target="_blank" >U.S. Border Patrol Cmdr. Gregory Bovino</a>.</p><p>In his most extensive commentary to date about Midway Blitz, Boutros acknowledged all did not go according to plan. Especially when it came to working with an “out of town”<b> </b>group of agents.</p><p>But Boutros said repeatedly, “We want to get it right.”</p><p>He made his remarks in a wide-ranging conversation with the Sun-Times and Chicago Tribune to mark Tuesday’s<b> </b>one-year anniversary of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/03/28/former-prosecutor-andrew-boutros-named-chicagos-interim-u-s-attorney" target="_blank" >the day he took office</a>.</p><h3><b>Federal prosecutors under ‘unprecedented pressures’</b></h3><p>Boutros, the son of immigrants from Egypt, is the first person of color to hold the office. He’s overtly patriotic, calls Chicago his “adopted”<b> </b>home<b> </b>and often drops references to Al Capone and the 1985 Chicago Bears.</p><p>His office moved quickly last year to level a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/11/19/terrorism-charges-filed-after-woman-lit-on-fire-on-blue-line-in-loop" target="_blank" >terrorism charge</a> against the man accused of setting<b> </b>a woman on fire on the Blue Line, and a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/02/chicago-crime-sheridan-gorman-loyola" target="_blank" >weapons charge</a> this month against the man separately charged with the murder of Loyola University Chicago freshman<b> </b>Sheridan Gorman.</p><p>Christopher Amon, the special agent in charge of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives in Chicago, said violent crime prosecutions “were not a priority”<b> </b>for the U.S. attorney’s office before Boutros’ arrival — and now that’s changed.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/05/30/andrew-boutros-interim-us-attorney-chicago-judges-role-pam-bondi" >Is Chicago’s interim U.S. attorney here to stay? Judges could play an unusual role in picking top prosecutor</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Boutros had kind words for controversial Attorney General Pam Bondi, even after Trump fired her<b> </b>last week. Bondi<b> </b>first appointed Boutros to his post, and Boutros said “she was tremendously good to Chicago and to this office.” The city’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2025/07/25/chicagos-federal-judges-choose-andrew-boutros-as-top-prosecutor" target="_blank" >federal judges later chose Boutros</a> to serve in the role on a more permanent basis.</p><p>Still, Boutros knows he has his critics, having once referred to their “sneering disbelief.”<b> </b>He’s endured grand jurors refusing to hand up indictments against at least three people — considered a rare rebuke. And a magistrate judge in November <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/20/unusual-and-possibly-unprecedented-judge-calls-out-chicago-feds-as-midway-blitz-cases-fall-apart" target="_blank" >questioned the charging decisions of Boutros’ office</a> during Midway Blitz, noting that “these are not ordinary times”<b> </b>at Chicago’s federal courthouse.</p><p>Defense attorneys are also exploring whether the White House <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >involved itself in the conspiracy case</a> brought by Boutros’ office against a group of Midway Blitz protesters known as the “Broadview Six.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f40000" name="image-f40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bbe1361/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9424784/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg Abughazaleh and three others are charged in a conspiracy to impede a federal agent outside the facility." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aeeca55/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff8ef2d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aeeca55/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Kat Abughazaleh, a member of the so-called Broadview Six, appears in a screenshot of footage from outside an immigration holding facility in Broadview. Abughazaleh and three others are charged in a conspiracy to impede a federal agent outside the facility. Prosecutors dropped charges against two others.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Ron Safer, a former federal prosecutor in Chicago who left the U.S attorney’s office in 1999, told the Sun-Times that the “unprecedented exodus of experience, talent and leadership” under Boutros “diminishes the office in ways that are obvious and ways that are not so obvious.”</p><p>He said things might be different if Boutros’ office appeared to be “resisting”<b> </b>a Justice Department that Safer said has “sprinted from its founding principles.”</p><p>But another office alum, Joe Thompson, said “unprecedented pressures” have been placed on U.S. attorney’s offices across the country. Thompson is the former high-ranking federal prosecutor who left office in Minnesota after the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/14/chicago-law-firm-representing-renee-nicole-goods-family-romanucci-blandin" target="_blank" >fatal shooting of Renee Good.</a></p><p>Now in private practice, Thompson’s clients include indicted journalist<b> </b>Don Lemon. He served in Chicago’s U.S. attorney’s office from 2009 until 2014, led Minnesota’s U.S. attorney’s office for several months in 2025 and served as second-in-command until shortly after Good was shot by an immigration officer.</p><p>“I think [Boutros] is in an incredibly difficult situation,” Thompson told the Sun-Times. “I think even [former U.S. Attorney] Patrick Fitzgerald would struggle to be U.S. attorney in the current environment, and Andrew certainly is having to navigate some rocky waters.”</p><h3><b>Blitz cases have collapsed — but more are coming</b></h3><p>There are “some very serious cases”<b> </b>still coming out of Midway Blitz, Boutros warned.</p><p>“You can’t commit a serious crime in this district and think that that’s OK,” he said. “There will be consequences.”</p><p>The handling of nonimmigration crimes tied to Midway Blitz is perhaps the most publicly prominent part of Boutros’ first year in office. Federal prosecutors charged<b> </b>32 known people with such crimes, but no one has been convicted. Twenty have already been cleared.</p><p>Federal prosecutors normally boast a conviction rate around 90%.<b> </b>They’re expected to bring charges only when they can prove them to a jury beyond a reasonable doubt.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-250000" name="image-250000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8fa22e6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F59%2F4ebdcd7e4d92b49cb5a32e0b6638%2Fblitztoll-260301-14.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fd52bd5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F59%2F4ebdcd7e4d92b49cb5a32e0b6638%2Fblitztoll-260301-14.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e65d438/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F59%2F4ebdcd7e4d92b49cb5a32e0b6638%2Fblitztoll-260301-14.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Jocelyne Robledo (left) and Ray Collins at their home in Englewood, Friday, Feb. 20, 2026. class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jocelyne Robledo (left) and Ray Collins are among the defendants who had their charges dropped after being targeted in prosecutions tied to Operation Midway Blitz.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Boutros explained that, after the first 10 days of Midway Blitz, his office realized “the protocols that we had applied in other cases and in other matters for decades and decades were not really well suited for the Blitz cases.”</p><p>Boutros said they then shifted from filing charges largely for “reactive arrests”<b> </b>by agents to an “investigate and then evaluate” approach.</p><p>He said the traditional protocols didn’t work because “we were dealing with an out-of-town group” of hundreds of agents who “weren’t accustomed to the Chicago procedures, Chicago training.” He declined to answer questions about trust in those agents.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/27/chicago-ice-trump-bovino-marimar-martinez-crime-border-patrol-midway-blitz" >ICE protesters keep beating Trump in Chicago court, but the battles take a toll — ‘It’s about intimidation’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“We had a visiting law enforcement group that came from the border, came several hundred strong, and they were brought into the city, and so there was obviously a difference of approach between maybe how they can do things — or do things — at the border,” Boutros said.</p><p>Crucially, Boutros said the workload for his office far exceeded the 32 known defendants. Not only did his team meet every morning “with the entire federal law enforcement apparatus in Chicago,” he said, but it reviewed “hundreds”<b> </b>of cases and wound up declining many charges.</p><p>A chart produced during Midway Blitz litigation<b> </b>shows that, among 92 people arrested by U.S. Customs and Border Protection<b> </b>between Sept. 2 and Oct. 29, charges were declined against 20 of them. That doesn’t include arrests made by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.</p><p>Midway Blitz also coincided with the longest<b> </b>government shutdown<b> </b>in history, Boutros noted.</p><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Our job was to ensure that there was law and order, peace, and that we respected First Amendment rights and other rights but that there was law and order that was happening, still, in the city,” Boutros said.</p><p>The result was “not perfect,”<b> </b>he said, but “not for lack of wanting to get it right.”<b> </b>He stressed that his team is willing to reconsider the cases it’s already charged based on “new video, new information, new facts, new equities.”</p><p>But there’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/27/chicago-ice-trump-bovino-marimar-martinez-crime-border-patrol-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >a cost to defendants who are charged</a> with federal crimes, even if those charges go away. A federal prosecution is intimidating. It carries the threat of prison time. Damaging news stories spread online.</p><p>Thompson said there’s “a real risk to the ongoing credibility of an office”<b> </b>when so many cases are dropped, abandoned or end in acquittals.</p><p>“It’s a significant red flag,”<b> </b>Thompson said.</p><h3><b>Resignations come with a cost</b></h3><p>Several veteran prosecutors left Boutros’ office in the last year, including nearly every criminal section chief. They took with them experience handling public corruption, violent crime and national security. Many of the section chiefs left later in Boutros' first year, after the start of Midway Blitz.</p><p>Boutros acknowledged it was a year of “transition.” But he touts the experience of a core team that includes First Assistant U.S. Attorney Jason Yonan, an 18-year veteran of the office; and criminal division chief Rick Young, a 24-year veteran.</p><p>Former prosecutors who left during Boutros’ first year either declined to comment for this article or did not respond to messages from the Sun-Times. Thompson, who left Minnesota’s U.S. attorney’s office in January, said everyone leaves “for his or her own reasons.”</p><p>“Some of those might be politics,” Thompson said. “Whether they disagree with the administration’s priority, or the U.S. attorney’s priorities. It could be more general: The type of case work they’re getting isn’t as professionally satisfying as they maybe had before.”</p><p>Regardless, he said it’s been happening around the country, in offices big and small.</p><p>Boutros explained that, “some people left because I told them I was going to demote them. Some people left because they came into my office and they said that they strongly dislike this administration and cannot work for this administration.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" >Marimar Martinez, shot by Border Patrol in Chicago, speaks out after Good, Pretti deaths: ‘I am their voice’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>"Some of them came to me and told me they were leaving because of the Blitz,” Boutros said.</p><p>But there’s a price to pay when so many veterans walk out the door, Safer said. It goes beyond institutional knowledge. When prosecutors leave, cases are reassigned, and he said that “causes at least substantial delay because a new attorney has to come up to speed.”</p><p>“It’s hard to overstate the impact of having assistants who are handling long-term investigations leave the office and have to hand it off to somebody else,” Safer said. “It results not only in justice being delayed, but at times in justice being denied.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a10000" name="image-a10000"></a> </div> </div><p>Boutros has praise for Amarjeet Bhachu, the ex-prosecutor seen as the driving force behind a massive corruption investigation that began in 2014. It led to the convictions of former powerhouse <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/12/21/24006969/ed-burke-verdict-chicago-corruption-bribery-danny-solis" target="_blank" >Ald. Edward M. Burke</a> in 2023 and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/madigan-trial-news/mike-madigan-guilty-verdict" target="_blank" >ex-Illinois House Speaker Michael J. Madigan</a> in February 2025.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-corruption-trials/2025/03/04/lead-madigan-prosecutor-leaving-u-s-attorney-office" target="_blank" >Bhachu left the U.S. attorney’s office</a> the month after Madigan’s conviction, before Boutros took office.</p><p>Boutros is otherwise critical of the office he inherited, though. He points to “historic lows”<b> </b>in “virtually every single metric that was tracked by the Department of Justice.” Statistics kept by the federal courts show cases were brought against 428 people here in 2024, down from 848 in 2020.</p><p>That number rose to 598 in 2025, most noticeably with a 115% increase in fraud cases. Boutros has also touted a large increase in cases brought with help from the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2023/9/27/23891321/chicago-crime-guns-atf" target="_blank" >Crime Gun Intelligence Center</a> run by the ATF.</p><p>Safer said such numbers “are largely misleading and irrelevant.” The FBI investigation that snared Burke and Madigan took several years and involved massive resources. The impact and significance of such a case is not reflected in the data.</p><p>“We need to be prosecuting organizations — as only the U.S. attorney’s office can do — who present a danger to the people of the Northern District of Illinois,” Safer said.</p><p>Boutros insists “you can do both.”<b> </b>He said an emphasis on “big cases”<b> </b>doesn’t explain the numbers he saw when he took over.</p><p>There are now 88 assistant U.S. attorneys on the criminal side of Boutros’ office in Chicago, and 25 on the civil side. There are 123 total, including in Rockford. But Boutros said his office is “on a major hiring spree,”<b> </b>with hopes of picking up “close to 50 new people” by the end of the year.</p><p>He said he’s had no trouble finding experienced applicants, contradicting the claim that Justice Department jobs have lost their luster.</p><h3><b>Boutros focused on violent crime and corruption</b></h3><p>There’s plenty of work for Chicago’s federal prosecutors to do beyond Midway Blitz. Boutros said his office is focused on fraud, human trafficking, child exploitation, violence, cartels, drugs, guns and gangs.</p><p>It leveled a terrorism charge in November against Lawrence Reed,<b> </b>the man accused of approaching a 26-year-old woman on a CTA Blue Line train, dousing her with gasoline and setting her on fire.</p><p>And last week,<b> </b>it filed a weapons charge against Jose Medina, the Venezuelan immigrant separately accused of the March 19 murder of Gorman, the 18-year-old Loyola University Chicago freshman. Boutros said he was taking “no chances”<b> </b>that Medina might walk free.</p><p>There are other initiatives, too. Boutros announced last year that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/04/cta-trains-violence-downtown-project-safe-neighborhoods" target="_blank" >Project Safe Neighborhoods would expand</a> to include “the entire rail system operated by the Chicago Transit Authority.” The program brings together federal, state and local law enforcement to identify pressing violent crime problems. It helped lead to the charges against Reed.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-230000" name="image-230000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fdfbb1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+89/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F94%2F75cb6ade468f874ed542354848f7%2Famon.jpeg" alt="Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives Chicago Field Division Special Agent in Charge Christopher Amon listens to a colleague during a recent meeting at the ATF office in Chicago." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fdfbb1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+89/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F94%2F75cb6ade468f874ed542354848f7%2Famon.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ebd1fff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+89/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F94%2F75cb6ade468f874ed542354848f7%2Famon.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fdfbb1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+89/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2F94%2F75cb6ade468f874ed542354848f7%2Famon.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Christopher Amon, special agent in charge of the Chicago field office of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, listens to a colleague during a meeting at the ATF’s Chicago office.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The ATF's Amon called Boutros a “relentless leader” who “cares deeply about his role in reducing violent crime”<b> </b>around Chicago.</p><p>Boutros created a health care fraud section and touts nearly<b> </b>$2 billion charged<b> </b>in such cases since taking office. His office has also been chosen as a “lead prosecutorial partner”<b> </b>on a Justice Department Trade Fraud Task Force, targeting people who evade federal customs laws.</p><p>He’s merged his office’s national security section with violent crimes — he says he can “surge resources into that unit as needed.” And he’s merged securities fraud with financial fraud.</p><p>Finally, there’s public corruption. Chicago’s U.S. attorney’s office has long been known for taking down some of the powerful politicians in this state, including governors, judges and lawmakers.</p><p>Turns out, Boutros said, “corruption has not stopped in Chicago.”</p><p>“Public corruption is a very special part of this office,” he said.</p><p>“And our work is not done.”</p>

entries.title: Sound & Gravity returns to Avondale, Bricktown over Labor Day weekend
entries.summary: <p>Locally-run, multi-venue festival <a class="Link" href="http://wbez.org/music/2025/09/08/pitchfork-mike-reed-sound-gravity-music-fest" target="_blank" ><u>Sound & Gravity</u></a> returns for its second edition over Labor Day weekend. The fest brings together more than 50 artists to perform across seven venues around Chicago’s Avondale and Roscoe Village neighborhoods Sept. 3-6, featuring genres such as jazz, experimental, contemporary classical and indie.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b90000" name="image-b90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5212549/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7052x3958+0+287/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2Fb5%2Fa94736e44ed2984b5bb40ee02c4f%2Fsym3397-third-coast-percussion.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/169f8ac/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7052x3958+0+287/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2Fb5%2Fa94736e44ed2984b5bb40ee02c4f%2Fsym3397-third-coast-percussion.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba835c5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7052x3958+0+287/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2Fb5%2Fa94736e44ed2984b5bb40ee02c4f%2Fsym3397-third-coast-percussion.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Third Coast Percussion performed for the inaugural Sound & Gravity Festival in 2025 at Rockwell on the River. It will be one of seven venues hosting this year’s performances.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Steve Sym</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The venues — Constellation, Hungry Brain, Beat Kitchen, Athenaeum Center, Judson & Moore Distillery, Rockwell on the River and Guild Row — are all located within two miles of one another, promoting a “choose your own adventure” style of festival. Sound & Gravity’s top billing includes psychedelic solo project Grouper, Contre Jour — a performance piece from former Sonic Youth guitarist Lee Ranaldo and experimental artist Leah Singer — post-rock band Son Lux, guitarist Marc Ribot and Ethiopian musician and composer Hailu Mergia. Colombian experimental artist Lucrecia Dalt, multi-instrumentalist and Grammy-winning producer Josh Johnson and Grammy-nominated drummer Kassa Overall are also slated to perform.</p><p>Notable local acts on the lineup include trumpeter and bandleader Will Miller’s Resavoir, melancholic soft-rock project Advance Base, underground ensemble Natural Information Society, alternative project Bitchin Bajas, Latin-influenced alt quartet Dos Santos, experimental ensemble microplastique and improvisational trio Twin Talk.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" venue Constellation Performing Arts, which was started by musician Mike Reed in 2013. Reed is also the co-founder of Pitchfork Music Festival, which ran in Chicago for 19 years <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2024/11/11/pitchfork-music-festival-wont-return-in-2025" target="_blank" ><u>before calling it quits in 2024</u></a>. Last year he told WBEZ that the goal of Sound & Gravity isn’t to take Pitchfork’s place, but to support Constellation and other indie music venues.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://soundandgravity.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Tickets are on sale now</u></a>, with four-day all-event passes starting at $240. Single-day passes start at $85 and Thursday opening night tickets at $50.</p>

entries.title: Former Bears great Steve McMichael diagnosed with CTE after death
entries.summary: <p>BOSTON — Hall of Famer Steve McMichael, a key member of the dominating defense that helped the 1985 Chicago <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> win the Super Bowl, has been diagnosed with chronic traumatic encephalopathy, the Concussion & CTE Foundation said.</p><p>McMichael <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/04/23/bears-steve-mcmichael-dies-obituary-als-hall-of-fame-defensive-lineman-nfl" target="_blank" >died last year</a> at 67 after a five-year fight with ALS.</p><p>"Too many NFL players are developing ALS during life and diagnosed with CTE after death," his wife, Misty McMichael, said in a statement released by the foundation. "I donated Steve's brain to inspire new research into the link between them."</p><p>CTE is a degenerative brain disease that has been found in athletes in contact sports, combat veterans and others exposed to repetitive head trauma. It has been known to cause violent mood swings, impulsive behavior and depression. It can only be diagnosed after death.</p><p>McMichael was inducted into the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2024/07/29/hall-of-fame-honor-is-huge-for-ailing-steve-mcmichael-but-he-didnt-get-there-alone" target="_blank" >Pro Football Hall of Fame</a> in 2024 after his ALS diagnosis prompted a push for the former defensive end, who finished his Bears career ranked second in franchise history in sacks. He had hoped to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2024/06/07/bears-legend-steve-mcmichael-wont-go-to-own-pro-football-hall-of-fame-ceremony" target="_blank" >travel to Canton</a> for enshrinement but his declining health wouldn't afford him that luxury; instead, he was awarded the honor in Homer Glen, surrounded by his former teammates. </p><p>When he <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/04/23/bears-steve-mcmichael-dies-obituary-als-hall-of-fame-defensive-lineman-nfl" target="_blank" >died last year</a>, former teammate Dan Hampton told the Sun-Times he wanted to remember he and McMichael "chasing down [quarterbacks] Tommy Kramer and Kenny Stabler and laughing like hyenas.”</p><p>According to a 2021 study by the Harvard Medical School and the Boston University CTE Center, NFL players are more than four times more likely to develop ALS than other men. Dr. Ann McKee, director of the Boston University CTE Center, said about 6% of individuals with CTE in the brain bank also have ALS, also known as Lou Gehrig's disease.</p><p>"There is strong evidence linking repetitive brain trauma and ALS," McKee said.</p><p>A two-time All-Pro and 2024 Pro Football Hall of Fame inductee, McMichael was known as "Mongo" and "Ming The Merciless" while playing in a franchise-record 191 consecutive games from 1981-93. McMichael played his final NFL season with Green Bay in 1994, and then went on to a career in pro wrestling.</p><p>In 2021, McMichael revealed he had ALS, which affects nerve cells in the brain and spinal cord, causing loss of muscle control. He followed the research seeking a link between ALS and CTE and pledged his brain to be studied, according to Chris Nowinski, co-founder and CEO of the Concussion & CTE Foundation.</p><p>"Steve McMichael was known for his strength, toughness, and larger-than-life presence, but his final act was to give a piece of himself back to the sports community so that we might have a chance to save ourselves," Nowinski said. "I appreciate all the former athletes, including many of Steve's '85 Bears teammates, who are raising funds and volunteering to participate in CTE research so we can create a brighter future for athletes everywhere."</p><p><i>Patrick Finley contributed. </i></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Chicago's U.S. prosecutor looks back on volatile year
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><h3></h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: Since taking office, U.S. Attorney Andrew Boutros has brought dozens of cases tied to the deportation blitz in Chicago — without securing any convictions. But he insists he isn't taking orders from Washington.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: An appeals panel calls for a new trial in the fatal police shooting of Harith Augustus, a messy day in sports, Jackson Park cherry blossoms arrive early and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-y/" target="_blank" >The Cubs lost to the Rays</a>, 6-4; <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-j/" target="_blank" >the White Sox fell to the Orioles</a>, 2-1; <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-t/" target="_blank" >the Blackhawks were bested by the Sharks</a>, 3-2.</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletter-hub.suntimes.com/" target="_blank" >Get this newsletter delivered to your inbox weekday mornings.</a></p><p>⏱️: <i>An 8-minute read</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>TODAY’S Boutros' first year also featured an "unprecedented exodus" of prosecutors, critics note. Most section chiefs left. And convictions have yet to emerge from the flurry of charges the office filed during <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-k/" target="_blank" >Operation Midway Blitz</a>.</p><p><b>Key quote</b>: When Chicago’s top federal prosecutor is asked if his office takes marching orders from Washington, he doesn’t mince words. "[There is] not a single case, OK, involving politics in our decision making. Full stop. Period. Zero," Boutros said. "And anyone who says otherwise is misstating reality. And anyone who says otherwise is an armchair expert who doesn’t know what they’re talking about. Period."</p><p><b>'Stay tuned'</b>: Boutros told the Sun-Times in a lengthy interview that he isn’t done with Midway Blitz. Nor with <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-u/" target="_blank" >public corruption</a> — he said people should "stay tuned."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>POLICE REFORM ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8f0000" name="image-8f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/40d5e80/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F67%2F529ab11d4f719621091a411a2276%2F1000391926.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1909276/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F67%2F529ab11d4f719621091a411a2276%2F1000391926.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a17d85b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F67%2F529ab11d4f719621091a411a2276%2F1000391926.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Demonstrators rally in the South Shore neighborhood protesting the shooting death of 37-year-old Harith Augustus on July 16, 2018 in Chicago, Illinois. target="_blank" >New trial ordered in lawsuit over deadly Chicago police shooting of Harith Augustus</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-n/" target="_blank" >Sophie Sherry</a></h5><p><b>Appeals panel</b>: A state appellate court panel has ordered a new trial to determine if a Chicago police officer acted with "utter indifference" when he fatally shot barber Harith Augustus in South Shore nearly eight years ago. The three judges concluded last week that Cook County Judge Bridget Hughes erroneously allowed city lawyers to dismiss two Black potential jurors during the initial trial, which stemmed from a lawsuit by Augustus’ family.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Augustus was fatally shot by Dillan Halley, a probationary police officer, on July 14, 2018. The city was still grappling with fallout from the fatal police shooting of Laquan McDonald at the time, and Augustus’ death prompted a fresh wave of protests. Augustus' family sued the city and the officers connected to the shooting a few months later, but the case took five years to get to trial. In July 2023, a jury ruled in favor of the officers.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>MORE NEWS YOU NEED 🗞️</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-31a99294-3282-11f1-abce-eb95c77ac527"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-x/" target="_blank" ><b>Teen mourned after hit and run</b></a>: The family of 18-year-old Damian Gomez, killed in a hit-and-run early Sunday, is reeling as Chicago police continue to look for the driver. href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-z/" target="_blank" >Last man at the shoeshine stand</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-v/" target="_blank" >Stefano Esposito</a></h5><p><b>Foot work</b>: Forty-dollar shoes. Thousand-dollar shoes. And everything in between. Steve Fullerton has cared for them all, the snap of his buffing rags echoing beneath the Cook County Building's marble ceiling for the past 20 years.</p><p><b>Last man's stand</b>: Standalone shoeshine guys like Fullerton are an endangered species. Still, he shows up each morning at 8:30 a.m. sharp, with a pack of Newport smokes, a can of ginger ale and a White Sox or Bears cap on his head. He keeps his gear locked up in a tall wooden box that looks like something a magician might use. R&B oldies crackle from a dusty radio.</p><p><b>Key quote</b>: You can tell a lot about a person by how they treat their shoes, says the 57-year-old West Sider. "If you don’t care for your shoes, what other parts of your life aren’t you taking care of?" Fullerton says. "You only live once. God gave you two feet. Be good to them. They take you all over. They walk for you. They help you dance."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-e/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>WATCH: MEET STEVE FULLERTON ▶️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="113" height="200" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/-7oMwCn33zE?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="One of the last shoeshine guys in Chicago"></iframe> </div> </div></div><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-w/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-31aa07c3-3282-11f1-abce-eb95c77ac527"><li>New national Census data show a near doubling of same-sex couple households. In the mix are 130 galleries from at least two dozen countries, down from last year’s nearly 200 featured galleries. As in previous years, the art show will set up inside Navy Pier’s Festival Hall. </p><p><b>Fair fare</b>: Admission for opening night is ticketed at $165 per person. Single-day admission tickets start at $40, and three-day admission tickets begin at $68. Guided tours can be booked for $30, and seniors and students may receive discounts. Children under 12 get in for free. </p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-jl/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-jk/" target="_blank" >Cherry blossoms are blooming — for a limited time</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-ju/" target="_blank" >Michael Puente</a></h5><p>Luying Deng missed seeing the cherry blossoms on Easter Sunday. So she made it a point Monday to head to Jackson Park before it’s too late.</p><p>She was not disappointed. She saw delicate pink and white flower petals.</p><p>"We’re lucky! It’s blooming," said Deng, of Chicago.</p><p>Blooming has begun for some of the 230 cherry trees surrounding the Columbia Basin south of the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry.</p><p>The robust bloom, expected to unfold over the next five to 10 days, has been fueled by fluctuations in weather and temperature, combined with an ample amount of rainfall, according to the Chicago Park District.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiylrjy-l-tl/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p><b>What's a clear sign of spring in Chicago?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Fans de la Universidad de Illinois lamentan derrota, pero terminan la temporada emocionados
entries.summary: <p>El ruido en Mullen’s Sports Bar and Grill en el vecindario de West Ridge llegó en oleadas. Se intensificó con cada enceste, cada tiro fallido y cada error defensivo durante la derrota de la Universidad de Illinois en las semifinales del sábado ante la Universidad de Connecticut (UConn).</p><p>"Es unificadora", dijo Kayla Mendoza, quien veía el partido junto a su hermana y su madre, una exalumna de la Universidad de Illinois-Champaign en el bar la noche del sábado. "Realmente nos estamos uniendo ahora".</p><p>No todo se unió para los Illini, como son apodados los jugadores del equipo de Illinois. Su remontada en la segunda mitad se quedó corta en una derrota de 71-62. Eso mantendrá a los aficionados esperando al menos otro año por el primer campeonato nacional de la escuela estatal insignia.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-090000" name="module-090000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Pero para docenas de clientes que vestían los colores naranja y azul marino que llenaron el bar para el partido, el futuro todavía se veía brillante.</p><p>Y desde luego no afectó negativamente el negocio en Mullen's, uniendo a aficionados y clientes habituales mientras el equipo lograba su mayor avance en dos décadas.</p><p>"Es una multitud diferente a la de un partido de la NCAA (Asociación Nacional Atlética Universitaria)", dijo Carrie Maw, la gerente de Mullen's. "Vienen muchos más exalumnos... Gente animada. Se divierten mucho".</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fac4f6" name="image-fac4f6"></a> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>La gente reacciona mientras observa un partido de semifinales del torneo de baloncesto universitario de la NCAA en la fase final entre UConn e Illinois, el sábado 4 de abril de 2026, en Mullen’s Sports Bar and Grill en Chicago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Para el medio tiempo, los aficionados estaban estresados pero aún optimistas mientras los Illini estaban detrás 37-29.</p><p>"Nuestra defensa no está defendiendo. No estamos marcando. Necesitamos detenerlos", dijo Mendoza. "No sé qué está pasando".</p><p>Deb Shawver, residente de Skokie y fanática de toda la vida, de 56 años —vestida con la vieja camisa de botones de Illinois de su padre— mantenía sus ojos pegados a la pantalla. Admiraba la presión que enfrentaban los jóvenes en el partido nacionalmente televisado.</p><p>"Es una locura porque estos muchachos son como atletas profesionales", dijo Shawver. "La presión está sobre ellos cuando tienen como 18, 19, 20 años".</p><p>A medida que los Illini estaban atrás al final de la primera mitad, Shawver mantenía su confianza. "Creo que sus nervios están calmados y están volviendo a entrar".</p><p>La multitud creció gradualmente en volumen mientras los Illini recortaban la desventaja a seis puntos con siete minutos restantes en el juego. Los gritos de "I-L-L" estallaron mientras el equipo buscaba cambiar la dinámica.</p><p>“Tenemos que conseguir los pin---- rebotes”, dijo Mendoza aún más frustrada a medida que el juego entraba en sus minutos finales. “Estoy esperanzada, estoy emocionada”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" </div><p>Algunos aficionados permanecieron de pie en anticipación mientras los Illini estaban atrás por cinco puntos con menos de tres minutos por jugar. Gemían y aclamaban mientras UConn e Illinois intercambiaban tiros de tres puntos.</p><p>“Vamos a hacer las cuentas”, dijo un cliente mientras intentaba calcular cómo los Illini podrían borrar una desventaja de dos posesiones con menos de un minuto restante.</p><p>Pero a medida que los segundos se terminaban para la temporada de Illinois, Karina Mendoa, la hermana de Kayla, no podía ocultar su decepción. “No vi el deseo de seguir luchando", dijo. "Connecticut se lo merecía, honestamente. Fueron un mejor equipo”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-33f5aa" entries.title: Sky extend core qualifying offer to guard Ariel Atkins
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> have extended a core qualifying offer to guard Ariel Atkins, according to the WNBA transactions page.</p><p>A core qualifying offer is a fully guaranteed one-year deal worth the supermax, which is reported to be $1.4 million in the new collective-bargaining agreement. </p><p>Atkins was the Sky’s third-leading scorer last season (13.1 points per game) and their best perimeter defender. She’s now an unrestricted free agent. </p><p>However, getting the core designation from the Sky means she can't negotiate with other teams in free agency.</p><p>The supermax would be attractive financially for Atkins, given those deals typically go to a select few superstars. It just depends whether Atkins wants to take a bet on a team that went 10-34 last season.</p><p>At the end of the dismal 2025 season, Atkins told the Sun-Times she was still undecided about her future.</p><p>The good news for the Sky is that even if Atkins declines the offer, she won’t be able to walk away for nothing. Per the rules of the core designation, she and the Sky can negotiate a move elsewhere via a sign-and-trade agreement.</p><p>That matters because the Sky traded the No. 3 pick in the 2025 draft to the Mystics to get Atkins. That pick became Sonia Citron, who was named to the All-Star team in her first season.</p><p>The deal didn't age well. The core designation is its saving grace.</p><p><b>Notes: </b>For the Sky to execute a sign-and-trade with Atkins, she would need to sign at a contract value lower than the supermax, per the rules of the new CBA.</p><p>The Sky also extended a reserved qualifying offer to point guard Sevgi Uzun. Uzun joined the Sky on a rest-of-season contract in 2025 and had a limited impact.</p>
