entries.title: Rachel Gansner, who started all-girls baseball league at North Side park, dies at 45
entries.summary: <p></p><p></p><p></p><p>Rachel Gansner, who started an all-girls fall baseball league in 2024 in Warren Park on the North Side, died Saturday after a long battle with stomach cancer. She was 45.</p><p>"Starting the league was an intense passion project of hers because she grew up loving the sport as a huge Cubs fan but wasn't able to play baseball as a kid because it wasn't an option for her," said her husband, Jeremy Gansner.</p><p>She started the all-girls league at Warren Park — located at Western Avenue and Pratt Boulevard — with about 25 players ranging in age from 6 to 9 that were spread among four teams: the Blue Sox, Comets, Peaches and Belles. </p><p>"Baseball is so hard. But when they finally had a hit or made a play or drove in a run, it wasn't just happiness, it was pride in themselves to see they were able to do it. And they kept getting better and better," said Jeremy Gansner.</p><p>"Rachel allowed them to feel that it's OK to fail, especially when your teammates are on the same page and support you and lift you up. And they became ballplayers. She really gave them the empowerment and tools to do that, and it's beautiful," he said.</p><p>Last year, the league expanded to form two age divisions — one for girls 6-9 and another for girls 10-12.</p><p>Mrs. Gansner's daughter, Eloise — known as Weezy — was in the league. Mrs. Gansner coached her team.</p><p>"Weezy blossomed into somebody who was confident and strong," said Jeremy Gansner. "It's going to be tough for her this year I think because one of her favorite things was having her mom as a coach. But her mom set her up for success."</p><p>The Gansner's 12-year-old son, George, helped out by serving as occasional umpire.</p><p>Jeremy Gansner kept score and did play-by-play announcing, occasionally handing the mic to kids who wanted to try their hand at announcers.</p><p>"The league is continuing this year," he said. "That's our hope and goal. I am going to work to make sure it continues."</p><p>Mrs. Gansner started playing baseball in a women's league in Chicago after college. She played second base and loved it immediately.</p><p>"She felt that it's a world where girls are pushed to softball, but it's not the same. It's the only sport where the male and female versions are different," Jeremy Gansner said.</p><p>When she started the all-girls league, Mrs. Gansner dropped pamphlets at North Side park district field houses, grocery stores, laundromats and coffee shops.</p><p>The infield of the baseball diamond they were going to use at Warren Park had become overgrown with weeds due to lack of use, and she spent many hours on her hands and knees over the course of several weeks, with the help of other volunteers, pulling them.</p><p>"She didn't want the girls to feel second rate with a weedy field," said Michael Phelps, who serves on the Warren Park Youth Baseball board. </p><p>Mrs. Gansner, who served as president of the board, also coached her son's team. </p><p>"She got us all to care and go along with her vision. She had that sort of force of character," said Steve Hendershot, a friend and volunteer coach.</p><p>"My daughter, Jade, plays baseball and she started on one of the co-ed teams at Warren Park that was mostly boys, but when she started in the all-girls league, all the sudden, she found her voice, her friends and got much better at baseball rapidly," Hendershot said.</p><p>Mrs. Gansner also founded a non-profit called Let's Play Too that focused on opening up the sport to girls.</p><p>Mrs. Gansner was born Dec. 15, 1980, to Judith Caplan, an education consultant, and Richard Caplan, an architect. She grew up in Lincoln Park playing softball and attended Francis W. Parker School. She later graduated from Hobart and William Smith Colleges and earned a master's in education from DePaul University.</p><p>"She was a friend, a deep, tight friend, and she loved parties in our backyard when the weather turned nice and it became home base for barbecues and fires in the fire pit and staying up late with lights and music and with the kids running around enjoying neighborhood," her husband said. </p><p>Mrs. Gansner, who lived in Edgewater, was a die-hard Cubs. She was at first a tad concerned about marrying a guy from Cleveland — whose hometown team faced the Cubs in the World Series in 2016.</p><p>"But we agreed this relationship would work early on because never ever would these two horrible teams meet in the World Series," Jeremy Gansner joked about their thought process at the time.</p><p>The Warren Park Youth Baseball Board plans to name a field after Mrs. Gansner, and the name of the all-girls league that she created will also bear her name beginning next season.</p><p>Funeral services have been held. A kickball game to take place at Warren Park is being planned to celebrate Mrs. Gansner's life.</p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Blackhawks' Kevin Korchinski hopes Chicago summer prepares him for full-time NHL play
entries.summary: <p>Every summer of offseason training has been important for young <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> defenseman Kevin Korchinski, but the upcoming one will be the most important yet.</p><p>That's because the AHL fallback option no longer exists for Korchinski and the Hawks. He will lose his waiver exemption next season, and since there's no way the 2022 No. 7 overall pick would go unclaimed on waivers, the Hawks won't consider it.</p><p>So that means it's NHL-or-bust now. He simply must figure out how to be a competent defenseman at this level, even if he's still a couple years away from reaching his peak.</p><p>He has a new plan for this summer, though, that might help him take a bigger developmental step forward during it than he has in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/04/09/blackhawks-kevin-korchinski-get-stronger-summer-nhl" target="_blank" >previous summers</a>, which he has spent at home in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan.</p><p>The new plan? Stay in Chicago.</p><p>"I haven't really experienced the full city of Chicago in the summer, and it's quite nice," Korchinski said Monday. "But I think it would be good just to work on more game-type stuff in Chicago and work with coaches closely. They've seen me play the past three years. They know what I need to work on and keep improving, and what my strengths are and how to keep pumping those strengths up."</p><p>Korchinski and other Hawks players aren't allowed to work with Jeff Blashill and the Hawks' official coaching staff during the offseason, but they can work with skills coach Brian Keane and strength coach Juan Gonzalez at Blackhawks Ice Center with approval from the league.</p><p>Alex Vlasic has done that for years, and Wyatt Kaiser did that for the last five or six weeks of last summer. Korchinski has seen their NHL success and hopes to follow the same path.</p><p>Kaiser thinks Korchinski might find the experience even more beneficial emotionally than physically.</p><p>"It's definitely a positive [to work with coaches], but it's more about making [Chicago] feel like home," Kaiser said. "On the road and during the season, you're all over the place, and it's stressful. You don't really get time.</p><p>"Being able to have a good month where you're training but you're [also] going around, getting food with the fiancée, I found I enjoyed it."</p><p>Blashill also thinks it's a good idea, although he isn't allowed to push that on anyone.</p><p>"For any of our players that want really focused attention, we have unreal resources in Chicago," Blashill said. "There's a lot of good skating and skills and strength coaches [out there], but we have a narrowed focus on what we want each guy to be better at."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Another strong game for Kevin Korchinski tonight. He logged a season-high 15:38: <a href="https://t.co/GzxL6A5K8J">pic.twitter.com/GzxL6A5K8J</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2041376205761388664?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 7, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Gaining strength without getting slower needs to be priority No. 1 for Korchinski. But it's encouraging he seems to be learning how to defend better with his body type.</p><p>He is a beautiful skater, and he's using that speed and acceleration to get to pucks first in the defensive zone and move them out of danger before taking contact. His passing created numerous rush chances for the Hawks <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/05/blackhawks-kraken-seattle-curse-arvid-soderblom-kevin-korchinski" target="_blank" >against the Kraken</a> and Sharks, too.</p><p>"[I'm] trying to use my feet in a smart way and be confident with my breakout passes, my gaps and killing plays in the 'D'-zone," Korchinski said. "Doing the simple things helps you play more in the 'O'-zone...[so you] don't have to spend as much time in the D-zone.</p><p>"In the past, I've definitely been too active where I'm skating myself away from plays, away from good ice. Being in good ice, that defensively prevents [opponents from] getting in those spots."</p><p>Over the last five NHL games since his latest call-up, Korchinski leads Hawks defensemen with a 49.0% scoring-chance ratio and has only been on the ice for one goal against, albeit in relatively sheltered minutes. </p><p>If he can consistently play like this next season, there will be no problem whatsoever.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Kevin Korchinski using his speed to make a good defensive play, then settling things down with a clean pass: <a href="https://t.co/OZPksnnjEH">pic.twitter.com/OZPksnnjEH</a></p>— Ben Pope’s Video Clips (@BenPopeCSTclips) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCSTclips/status/2041602223029805108?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 7, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Four sought in fatal bludgeoning of man, 25, after fight in Avondale tavern
entries.summary: <p>Chicago police are asking for the public's help in identifying four people possibly linked to the fatal bludgeoning of a 25-year-old man who died after a fight in a bar in Avondale last month.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/01/death-of-25-year-old-avondale-man-ruled-homicide-from-assault" target="_blank" >Alexander C. Kazanowski, 25, was found unresponsive</a> on the ground outside at 3942 W. Belmont Ave., with head injuries about 3:20 a.m. on March 24, Chicago police said.</p><p>Detectives learned that Kazanowski earlier had been "forcibly removed" from the Tune Up Bar, 3910 W. Belmont Ave., after a fight there, according to a police report.</p><p>He was taken to Advocate Illinois Masonic Medical Center in critical condition. Kazanowski, of the 4900 block of West Nelson Avenue, was later pronounced dead at 3:31 p.m., March 28th, the Cook County medical examiner’s office said.</p><p>An autopsy on March 29th determined Kazanowski died of blunt head trauma from an assault, and his death was ruled a homicide, according to the medical examiner's office. </p><p>Anyone with information about the attack or those pictures is urged to contact detectives, (312) 746-6614 or submit an anonymous tip at CPDTIP.com and use reference RD # JK192078. </p><p>Area 5 detectives are investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Trial set for Chicago woman accused of killing boyfriend after baby shower — despite calls to drop charges
entries.summary: <p>A trial date was set Tuesday for a Chicago woman accused of fatally stabbing her boyfriend, despite her attorneys arguing she acted in self-defense while she was pregnant.</p><p>Keshia Golden's attorneys have argued that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/24/chicago-crime-keshia-golden-domestic-violence-abuse-murder" target="_blank" >her boyfriend, Calvin Sidney, grew violent</a> after a shower for their first child in October 2022, slamming Golden into a counter and a fridge while she was eight months pregnant. </p><p>Officials have said she grabbed a knife and stabbed Sidney in the leg, hitting his femoral artery and causing his death. Golden faces faces two counts of first-degree murder.</p><p>The Cook County state's attorney's office offered a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/03/chicago-keshia-golden-crime-domestic-violence-murder-eileen-oneill-burke" target="_blank" >plea deal</a> last month to lesser charges of second-degree murder in exchange for two years of probation. But Golden’s attorneys said accepting the deal would have lasting consequences, requiring her to register as a violent offender, limiting her access to benefits and impacting her employment opportunities.</p><p>With no deal struck, Cook County Judge Steven Watkins on Tuesday set a trial date of Aug. 17. Golden has been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/11/4/23441087/pregnant-woman-murder-charge-community-group-provides-bond-keshia-golden" target="_blank" >out of jail while awaiting trial</a>, thanks to her team of lawyers and advocates. She was able to give birth and raise her daughter, Ky’liyah.</p><p>Her backers filled the courtroom in a sea of purple, hoping prosecutors would drop the charges Tuesday. Instead, they were disappointed by the decision to move forward.</p><p>Kyle Keenan, one of Golden's attorneys, said prosecutors "had another opportunity to do the right thing in this case and refused to dismiss the charges."</p><p>“The message that the state is sending is that if you defend yourself, you will be punished,” he said.</p><p>A status hearing was scheduled for July 13.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/24/chicago-crime-keshia-golden-domestic-violence-abuse-murder" target="_blank" >Chicago woman says she killed abusive boyfriend to save herself and their unborn child. Will a jury agree?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/03/chicago-keshia-golden-crime-domestic-violence-murder-eileen-oneill-burke" target="_blank" >Probation offered to woman accused of killing abusive boyfriend — but her lawyers want case dropped</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/11/4/23441087/pregnant-woman-murder-charge-community-group-provides-bond-keshia-golden" target="_blank" >Pregnant woman facing murder charge to be released from jail after community group provides bond</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: For Ye, embracing Nazism not the surest path to re-establishing parental rights
entries.summary: <p>Did you notice, in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/04/05/nasa-space-moon-artemis" target="_blank" >Monday’s column</a>, how I copped to not knowing about the Greek god Artemis before the current moon mission? Despite all my talk about being educated. How can I do that? Because one of the things I learned is that the world is big, filled with stuff, and most people know absolutely nothing about almost everything. The shame is in pretending otherwise.</p><p>So I can confess that it wasn't until Monday, reading my Sun-Times with my morning Nespresso, that I learned, on page 12, that Ye, the artist formally known as Kanye West, last year released a song called "Heil Hitler." If, like me, you just found out about it now, what is your reaction? Are you offended? Incredulous? I hope not. As one of my cherished readers, I'd prefer your reaction to be curiosity, mirroring my own response: an enthusiastic, "I gotta hear this song."</p><p>Easier said than done. One of the worst aspects of our current dilemma is the idea of gatekeepers preventing supposedly vulnerable populations from having their sensibilities seared. Whether the right, vindictively trying to purge life of people they hate. Or the left, timidly trying to pretend that hate doesn't exist. One is worse; neither are commendable.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-690000" name="module-690000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>I started at YouTube. Nothing but criticisms and parodies. Then Apple Music. The same. So I did a Google search, and found the full song — of course — on that slop sink of hate, X, nee Twitter.</p><p>I stopped using X regularly when Elon Musk went full fascist — his Nazi salute. Kind of a giveaway. But I didn't quit, for eventualities like this.</p><p>"With all of the money and fame I still can't get my kids back," Ye trills. "So I became a Nazi, yeah."</p><p>Stop right there. Offended yet? Of course not. At this point, if you are like me, you feel sorry for Ye, who has four children with his former wife Kim Kardashian. </p><p>Imagine connecting those two thoughts — complaining about not being able to see your children, then using that as an excuse to embrace Nazism. Is Ye expecting that to help? "Your honor, I need to see my kids. I know I had troubles in the past — never should grabbed that microphone from Taylor Swift. But I've worked hard to improve myself. I'm a Nazi now..."</p><p>Not a smart strategy, right?</p><p>I shouldn't jest. Ye has admitted to being bipolar, and nobody argued with him. He also apologized for the song, though that's a tough one to claw back. He also sold Nazi merchandise. </p><p>I think it's important to recognize that people still embrace the Nazis. It's valuable to be reminded of their error, which sadly is not confined to the 1930s. To embrace Nazism is to be lulled by a strong start — great uniforms, bold iconography, massive Nuremberg rallies, the Blitzkrieg, those diving Stukas — but ignore the bad end. Your nation bombed to total ruin, the Nuremberg trials.</p><p>Any more recent examples of the error of allowing a strong start to conceal a bad finish? Maybe being sunk into a military morass by the illusion of bombing an enemy who, when the smoke clears, is somehow still there, still closing the Strait of Hormuz. That comes to mind.</p><p>I don't think people should be prevented from expressing the hate within their hearts; I think they should be compelled to. So they can face the consequences — and haters almost inevitably hurt themselves more than anybody else. Ye was going to headline some big summer festival in London, until the sponsors started pulling out. If I were a Brit, I might not be a fan of the idea that our prime minister should vet concert artists. Yet we are allowed to draw away in disgust from those who are revolting — the far right pretends their free speech is being trampled. Elon Musk sued companies who didn't want to see their names floating around in his toilet. But it's only fair: if haters can rant, decent people can avert their faces.</p><p>Then again, "fairness" is not high among hater values.</p><p>Admiring the Nazis is betting on a team that has already lost. It's like envying someone jumping out of a plane without a parachute. The thrilling view! The wind in their hair! Those smitten don't consider the splat at the end. I try to tell the Nazis who write in, waving this or that flimsy fig leaf, "You know, the whole Nazi thing didn't work out so well for the Germans." They never engage. They always scurry off, as if I've raised a concept too deep for them to fathom. Maybe I have. Another reason to be willing to admit not knowing things. Given what others aren't aware of, we're still way ahead of the game.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Pritzker wants 25th Amendment invoked to remove President Trump from office over Iran threats
entries.summary: <p>Gov. JB Pritzker on Tuesday joined a growing chorus of lawmakers and conservative pundits calling for President Donald Trump's removal from office for doubling down on graphic threats to bomb Iran.</p><p>Trump on Thursday wrote on social media that, "A whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought again. I don't want that to happen, but it probably will." That message came after an Easter Sunday post that threatened to bomb Iran's electric grid and bridges as he demanded that the government in Tehran reopen the Strait of Hormuz. </p><p>“Tuesday will be Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day, all wrapped up in one, in Iran,” Trump wrote on Sunday. "Open the F*****’ Strait, you crazy bastards, or you’ll be living in Hell — JUST WATCH. Praise be to Allah.”</p><p>The Illinois governor, who is widely seen as a potential 2028 presidential contender, responded to Trump's latest threat by calling him "a deranged man threatening to wipe out an entire country."</p><p>"It's past time," Pritzker wrote on social media. "The 25th Amendment must be invoked." </p><p>Pritzker joined an unlikely alliance of conservatives calling for the president's removal, including former U.S. Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene, former MAGA ally and conservative radio host Alex Jones, and conservative commentator Candace Owens. Podcaster Tucker Carlson is also calling on Trump's Cabinet to reject any plan that would lead to the deaths of Iranian citizens. </p><p>Trump campaigned on a pledge of "no new wars," but has said the war in Iran is necessary to stop leaders there from obtaining nuclear weapons. That reversal is reverberating throughout the MAGA world. </p><p>U.S. Rep. Delia Ramirez, D-Illinois, on Thursday called Trump a "warmonger, escalating the conflict for his own profit and consolidation of power."</p><p>"My Republican colleagues can't keep turning a blind eye," Ramirez wrote on social media. "He must be stopped and impeached."</p><p>And Sen. Dick Durbin wrote, "When will Congressional Republicans stand up to President Trump’s risky war of choice?"</p><p>Pritzker in October also called on the 25th Amendment to be invoked after Trump posted a fake-AI video on Truth Social, with the governor writing, "there is something genuinely wrong with this man."</p><h3>What is the 25th Amendment? </h3><p>Section 4 of the Constitution's 25th Amendment has never been invoked, but it would allow Vice President JD Vance and a majority of the Cabinet to transfer "an incapacitated President's powers and duties" to the vice president, according to the Library of Congress. It can be invoked "when the President is unable or unwilling to declare that he is incapacitated." Vance and a majority of the Cabinet would have to send a written declaration to the president pro tempore of the Senate and the house speaker stating that the president is unable to discharge his office's powers and duties. Vance would immediately assume the powers and duties as acting president. </p><p>The other sections of the 25th Amendment include removing the president from office if he dies or resigns and making the vice president the president. Another section states that if there is a vacancy in the office of the vice president, the president would nominate a vice president to take office upon confirmation by a majority vote of both chambers of Congress. It also declares that if the president sends the president pro tempore of the Senate and the house speaker a written declaration that he is unable to discharge the powers and duties of his office, powers and duties would be transferred the vice president — until the president declares in writing that they can resume their duties. </p><p>According to the National Constitution Center, the temporary transfer happened three times albeit briefly when Ronald Reagan and George W. Bush told Congress the vice president would be acting as president while they were under general anesthesia for medical procedures. </p><h3>Can it be used to remove Trump from office? </h3><p>Neither Vance nor any other member of Trump's Cabinet has expressed support for removing Trump from office, so the chances are unlikely. </p><p>However, if it is invoked the president can fight back and notify Congress that no "inability" exists and resume powers until the vice president Cabinet jointly object. Congress would have to convene within 48 hours once the 25th Amendment is invoked and would have 21 days to discuss whether an inability exists. The vice president and Cabinet members would have four days to file an objection to the president's declaration within that time frame. If no objection is filed, the president would resume his duties. If it's contested, two-thirds of the House and Senate would have to agree to allow the vice president to serve as president. The president would also be able to file another declaration about ability to serve after Congress votes.</p><p>The Supreme Court has not definitively interpreted the 25th Amendment, and legal scholars are still debating the definition of presidential inability, according to the National Constitution Center.</p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Boy, 16, killed in South Shore drive-by
entries.summary: <p>A drive-by shooting in the South Shore neighborhood left a 16-year-old boy dead Tuesday morning. </p><p>The boy was in the 7800 block of South Coles Avenue when someone in a passing white vehicle with several people inside it opened fire, hitting him about 9:15 a.m., police said. </p><p>He was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center, where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>No arrests have been made. </p><p>Area 2 detectives are investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Gangster Disciples founder Larry Hoover has wife, ex-NY mob prosecutor push parole board to free him
entries.summary: <p>A former New York mob prosecutor stood up Tuesday for Larry Hoover, the king of the Gangster Disciples for decades in Chicago.</p><p>John Gleeson said he prosecuted criminals similar to Hoover — people like John Gotti, the notorious "Teflon Don" and boss of New York's Gambino crime family.</p><p>But Hoover is different, Gleeson said. He's "grown up" and is remorseful, Gleeson told the Illinois Prisoner Review Board, which is considering the 75-year-old former gang leader's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-gangs/2025/10/22/larry-hoover-gangster-disciples-founder-state-clemency-petition-jb-pritzker" target="_blank" >request for clemency</a> from Gov. JB Pritzker. </p><p>"Larry Hoover poses no risk of danger to the community," said Gleeson, who became a federal district judge and a member of the U.S. sentencing commission and is part of Hoover's legal team.</p><p>Dozens of people, including Hoover's wife Winndye, attended a prisoner review board hearing in downtown Chicago where a three-member panel heard testimony from Hoover's supporters, along with prosecutors who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/21/larry-hoover-clemency-jb-pritzker-eileen-oneill-burke-gangster-disciples" target="_blank" >oppose his freedom</a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-620000" name="image-620000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8b20f67/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2Fd5%2Ff7035df04c9cb0fd121a0fda73c5%2Fhoover-260408-7.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/792ef7e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2Fd5%2Ff7035df04c9cb0fd121a0fda73c5%2Fhoover-260408-7.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Former New York mob prosecutor John Gleeson (left) testifies Tuesday before the Illinois Prisoner Review Board as Larry Hoover’s lawyer, Justin Moore, and Hoover’s wife, Winndye, look on.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>One of them, Assistant Cook County State's Attorney Enrique Abraham, said a grant of clemency for Hoover would ignore the seriousness of the killing he's locked up for, would minimize the crimes he later committed in prison and would put the public in danger.</p><p>Abraham said Hoover ordered the 1973 killing of William "Pooky" Young "to show everyone that he would protect his criminal enterprise at all costs.” Even though he's now aging, his freedom would still pose a risk because "his criminal acts have almost always been carried out vicariously through surrogates," according to Abraham.</p><p>"William Young never got a second chance," Abraham said.</p><p>Despite that opposition, Hoover and his supporters have increasing hope for his release despite decades of rejections in the state's separate parole process. That's because Hoover won <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/05/28/president-trump-drug-kingpin-larry-hoover-commutation-federal-prison-sentence" target="_blank" >a separate grant of clemency</a> last year from President Donald Trump for a federal drug conspiracy sentence.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/05/29/larry-hoover-gangster-disciples-mar-a-lago-donald-trump-chicago-drug-kingpin" target="_blank" >Chicago drug kingpin Larry Hoover’s path to a commutation ran through Trump’s Mar-a-Lago resort</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Last summer, Hoover was freed from the federal "supermax" prison in Colorado and transferred to a different prison to serve his state murder sentence. Authorities said they won’t reveal the location of where he’s being held because of security concerns.</p><p>The state's prisoner review board holds hearings both for requests for parole and for executive clemency. </p><p>The board will consider the testimony given Tuesday and forward a confidential clemency recommendation to the governor, who makes the final decision. The panel typically takes about two months to give a recommendation to the governor.</p><p>The board didn't hear from Hoover, who wasn't at Tuesday's hearing. His wife Winndye told the board her family is ready for him to come home to Chicago after he missed his children's high school and college graduations and other momentous occasions.</p><p>"He's not a monster," she said. "His family loves him."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9c0000" alt="Gangster Disciples leader Larry Hoover (center) with top gang lieutenant Gregory Shell (left) and gang associate Keith McCain." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/39e1059/2147483647/strip/true/crop/468x263+0+151/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F37%2Fbd%2F0a07e22fc47cefe370d3ec75fcb7%2Fscreen-shot-2020-03-17-at-10-56-18-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/273d61e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/468x263+0+151/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F37%2Fbd%2F0a07e22fc47cefe370d3ec75fcb7%2Fscreen-shot-2020-03-17-at-10-56-18-am.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/39e1059/2147483647/strip/true/crop/468x263+0+151/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F37%2Fbd%2F0a07e22fc47cefe370d3ec75fcb7%2Fscreen-shot-2020-03-17-at-10-56-18-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gangster Disciples founder Larry Hoover (center) with top gang lieutenant Gregory Shell (left) and gang associate Keith McCain.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. District Court</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Hoover's lawyer Justin Moore acknowledged his client's criminal past. </p><p>Hoover ran a $100 million-a-year drug operation, and his gang expanded to more than 30,000 members in Chicago and across the country, authorities say. It been tied to countless killings.</p><p>"We are not here to excuse what happened in Mr. Hoover’s life," Moore said. "We are here to talk about accountability."</p><p>Moore echoed other supporters in saying Hoover reformed his life in federal prison, where he was held since the late 1990s until his recent move to the state prison system. </p><p>"Justice too long delayed is justice denied," Moore told the board, invoking the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr.</p><p>Hoover and fellow gang member Andrew Howard were both sentenced to 150-200 years in state prison for Young's killing. They also were suspected in the fatal shooting of a man who cooperated with Chicago police in the investigation of Young's murder.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/21/larry-hoover-clemency-jb-pritzker-eileen-oneill-burke-gangster-disciples" target="_blank" >Ex-Gangster Disciples boss Larry Hoover’s freedom bid should be rejected, Cook County top prosecutor says</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Howard was paroled in 1992. </p><p>Moore said Howard's freedom and Hoover's continued incarceration highlights the unfairness of the criminal justice system. </p><p>Moore also pointed out that Hoover's prison term was handed down under a state sentencing structure that no longer exists.</p><p>In the late 1990s, Hoover and his top leaders were convicted in federal court in a drug conspiracy case. Hoover was caught on a hidden microphone directing the street operations of the gang while he was in state prison.</p><p>Hoover was then moved to federal prison to serve a life sentence and spent much of that time in isolation in the supermax prison in Florence, Colo. Even though Trump granted Hoover clemency in his federal drug case, his state murder conviction has remained intact.</p><p>In a court filing, Hoover's lawyers said he renounced his ties to the Gangster Disciples and that his continued imprisonment is a “slow, state-sanctioned death sentence.” </p><p>“Hoover did not create the fire,” the petition said. “He grew up in it."</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Supporters of Gangster Disciples founder Larry Hoover raise their hands during a clemency hearing before the Illinois Prisoner Review Board on Tuesday at the Bilandic Building.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Among those who have backed Hoover’s bid for freedom is Arne Duncan, former President Barack Obama's secretary of education. Duncan now runs Chicago CRED, an anti-violence organization. Other supporters have included Chance the Rapper, the late Rev. Jesse Jackson Sr. and the Rev. Al Sharpton.</p><p>Board member Matthew Coates presided over Tuesday's clemency hearing alongside members Carmen Terrones and Jeffrey Grubbs. They didn't indicate how they're leaning on Hoover's request.</p><p>In 2024, the prisoner review board was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2024/03/25/parole-official-quits-after-approving-release-of-felon-who-then-stabbed-ex-girlfriend-killed-her-11-year-old-son" target="_blank" >thrown into turmoil</a> when two members quit after approving the release of a felon who later stabbed his former girlfriend and killed her son. The tragedy prompted Pritzker to sign a law that reformed the board, which now has nine members and six vacancies.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2024/03/25/parole-official-quits-after-approving-release-of-felon-who-then-stabbed-ex-girlfriend-killed-her-11-year-old-son " target="_blank" >2 parole officials quit after released felon allegedly stabs ex-girlfriend, kills her 11-year-old son</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/05/21/illinois-lawmakers-approve-reforms-prisoner-review-board" target="_blank" >Spurred by Crosetti Brand case, Illinois lawmakers approve reforms to the Prisoner Review Board </a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Frank Main

entries.title: Housing advocates to sue CHA over CEO appointment
entries.summary: <p>Housing advocates plan to sue the Chicago Housing Authority over its decision to hire a new CEO last month. </p><p>The CHA Board of Commissioners voted to appoint Keith Pettigrew, executive director of the District of Columbia Housing Authority, during a board meeting on March 17. Before the vote, <a class="Link" href="https://thecha.legistar.com/MeetingDetail.aspx?ID=1345705&GUID=C1EF2F3E-3485-48A3-9056-97C4F93224F8&Search=" target="_blank" >the agenda did not say</a> board members would be voting on the new CEO. The final agenda item was simply: "Approval of Personnel Matters." </p><p>In response, a group of advocates from the Lugenia Burns Hope Center, Working Family Solidarity and the Chicago Housing Initiative announced on Tuesday at a news conference outside CHA's headquarters their intent to file a lawsuit. </p><p>“There was not adequate or proper notification to the public about what they were doing. We believe this was done intentionally and deceptively,” said Roderick Wilson, executive director of the Lugenia Burns Hope Center. </p><p>The CHA had been without a CEO for 16 months. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/17/chicago-housing-authority-appoints-keith-pettigrew-as-new-ceo" target="_blank" >The board voted 7-2</a> to appoint Pettigrew. It was a victory over Mayor Brandon Johnson, who has long pushed for retired West Side Ald. Walter Burnett to lead the agency.</p><p>Cristina Pacione-Zayas, Johnson’s chief of staff, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/03/cristina-pacione-zayas-brandon-johnson-chicago-housing-authority-ceo-appointment-keith-pettigrew-matthew-brewer-walter-burnett" target="_blank" >said last Friday</a> there would be "consequences" for the board's decision. She alleged the board “knowingly” violated its “oath to uphold the law of the land locally, state as well as federal.”</p><p>Wilson said he has been in touch with the mayor's office for assistance on their planned lawsuit, but did not say Johnson had formally endorsed their effort. One of the supporters of the suit — but not a plaintiff — is People Matter, a nonprofit co-led by Consuela Hendricks, one of Johnson’s two new proposed appointees to the CHA board. </p><p>The suit is represented by Loevy & Loevy, and Wilson said they hope to file the complaint by Thursday or Friday. (Loevy & Loevy is representing Chicago Public Media in a lawsuit against the CHA for documents that the housing authority refused to disclose about CHA Commissioner <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/27/cha-commissioner-debra-parker-drops-lawsuit-against-the-housing-authority-ending-fight-to-keep-voucher" >Debra Parker‘s voucher case.</a>)</p><p>The housing advocates plan to allege in their complaint that the CHA board did not follow the Open Meetings Act, which dictates how government agencies should conduct their public meetings. </p><p>Last week, CHA Board Chair Matthew Brewer <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/03/cristina-pacione-zayas-brandon-johnson-chicago-housing-authority-ceo-appointment-keith-pettigrew-matthew-brewer-walter-burnett" target="_blank" >told the Chicago Sun-Times</a> he was “very confident” that the CHA board he chairs “complied with all applicable laws.” Brewer did not immediately respond to a request for comment Tuesday. </p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Muerte en Chicago Lawn de adolescente 'muy querido' marca la segunda fatalidad ciclista del año
entries.summary: <p>La familia de un joven de 18 años asesinado en un accidente de atropello y fuga la madrugada del domingo está conmocionada mientras la Policía de Chicago continúa buscando al conductor.</p><p>La muerte del adolescente, que la familia identificó como Damián Gómez, es al menos la segunda fatalidad de ciclistas en lo que va del año, dijeron las autoridades.</p><p>"Damián sólo tenía 18 años, tratando de encontrar su camino y sus logros futuros", escribió la tía del chico, Daisy Torres, en una publicación de GoFundMe. "Era un buen joven y muy querido entre todos nosotros".</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-7f0000" name="module-7f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/support-for-damians-family-after-tragedy" target="_blank" >GoFundMe</a> ha recaudado más de $12,500 hasta la mañana del martes.</p><p>El accidente ocurrió alrededor de la 1:20 a.m. cuando el conductor de una Ford Explorer roja 2015 viajaba a alta velocidad en la cuadra 6300 al sur de Kedzie Avenue, según la Policía de Chicago. El conductor se pasó un semáforo en rojo y atropelló a Gómez, que iba en bicicleta, antes de chocar con un Honda Civic plateado 2006 que viajaba hacia el sur, informaron las autoridades.</p><p>El impacto hizo que el automóvil plateado aterrizara sobre Damián Gómez, según un reporte de policía y familiares.</p><p>Gómez, de 18 años, fue declarado muerto en la escena, afirmó la policía. Una autopsia realizada el lunes fue inconclusa, a la espera de más estudios, según la Oficina del Médico Forense del Condado de Cook.</p><p>Una mujer de 22 años que era pasajera en el vehículo SUV rojo sufrió trauma por el accidente y fue llevada al Advocate Christ Medical Center en Oak Lawn, donde se encontraba en estado grave, según el informe. La mujer de 60 años que conducía el Civic plateado fue atendida en la escena.</p><p>El conductor del SUV huyó del lugar a pie antes de que los oficiales llegaran, según la policía. No hay nadie bajo custodia.</p><p>Es al menos la segunda vez que un ciclista ha muerto en un accidente este año, según datos del Departamento de Transporte de Chicago (CDOT) y la oficina del médico</p><p>forense.</p><p>El 24 de enero, un hombre de 63 años que montaba en bicicleta en la cuadra 8600 al oeste de Irving Park Road fue atropellado por un vehículo, según la oficina del médico forense. El ciclista falleció unos días después, el 29 de enero, según la misma fuente.</p><p>Su muerte y la de Gómez ocurren en un momento en que la ciudad ha visto una disminución en las muertes de ciclistas en accidentes. Chicago reportó dos muertes de ciclistas en 2025 —una cifra que ya se ha igualado en 2026— lo que representa una caída constante desde un pico de 11 en 2021. Entre 2010 y 2019, la ciudad tuvo un promedio de seis muertes de ciclistas por año, según el CDOT.</p><p>Esto a pesar de que el ciclismo aumentó un 119% entre el otoño de 2019 y la primavera de 2023 en Chicago, más que en cualquier otra gran ciudad estadounidense en los últimos cinco años, <a class="Link" href="https://www.replicahq.com/post/measuring-chicagos-boost-in-biking" target="_blank" >según un análisis patrocinado por el Departamento de Transporte de Chicago</a>.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: The bright side of the Angel Reese trade still looks dim
entries.summary: <p>The bright side of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/06/sky-trade-angel-reese-to-atlanta-dream-for-2027-2028-draft-picks" >Angel Reese trade</a> is that the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> can toss their "Twin Towers" strategy, which wasn't really working.</p><p>In 2024, the Sky drafted post players Reese and Kamilla Cardoso to lay the foundation for the franchise's future.</p><p>Both can score inside and dominate the glass, but neither entered the pros as an outside shooter. The Sky expected that to evolve.</p><p>Through two seasons, it didn't. They still scored most of their points in the paint and took very few shots from the perimeter. This shrunk the floor and made the offense predictable.</p><p>Meanwhile, the league was moving in the other direction. The most successful teams play with "stretch" bigs who can shoot from outside, forcing defenders into tough choices and opening driving lanes for everyone else.</p><p>Last season's top four teams in the regular-season standings — the Lynx, Aces, Dream and Mercury — averaged 7.1 3-point attempts per game from their post players.</p><p>Reese and Cardoso combined for 0.8.</p><p>The Sky knew this wasn't sustainable. Coach Tyler Marsh and general manager Jeff Pagliocca want to play a pace-and-space system that requires its bigs to let it fly.</p><p>With Reese gone to the Dream, they can target a stretch big in free agency to fulfill that vision immediately.</p><p>The problem is that the Sky don't have a compelling pitch for why the best ones should join them.</p><p>They've failed to construct a competitive roster the last two seasons. They've fallen behind the league's new standard in facilities and staffing.</p><p>And their best players in recent years — Kahleah Copper, Marina Mabrey and Courtney Williams — witnessed that dysfunction up close and left.</p><p>Does that sound like the kind of set-up that superstar free agents would consider, in the unlikely event that they choose to take their talents elsewhere? (The Sky are skeptical themselves that many superstars will be on the move this offseason, according to a source familiar with the team's thinking.)</p><p>Step down to the next tier of stretch bigs — Azurá Stevens, Alanna Smith — and the picture doesn't get much brighter. The Sky can try to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/03/26/skys-free-agency-path-runs-through-the-next-tier-not-the-stars" >woo them with a big payday</a>, but they're not the only team flush with cap space, and certainly not the only team hungry for their skill set. Stevens and Smith will have plenty of suitors to choose from.</p><p>One way to look at the Reese trade: The Sky gave up one of the league's most exciting young stars on a team-friendly contract, just to chase solid veterans they may not land.</p><p>There's a subtler dark side to this ordeal, too, which is that Reese's stagnation as a shooter reflects poorly on the Sky's player development operation.</p><p>Reese was hungry to extend her range throughout both seasons in Chicago. Yet her shot profile and mechanics remained largely unchanged year over year.</p><p>Why couldn't Marsh and Pagliocca — who came billed as elite player developers — help?</p><p>Maybe two years with the Sky, and only one under Marsh, is too short a window to judge.</p><p>But Reese's new coach has shown it can happen pretty fast.</p><p>Dream coach Karl Smesko needed just one year to turn forward Naz Hillmon into a legitimate long-range threat. After attempting just six 3-pointers across her first three seasons, Hillmon took 165 last year, hit 32.1% and won Sixth Player of the Year.</p><p>If Reese's range suddenly blossoms in Atlanta, Smesko's reputation as a developer will grow stronger. The Sky's will take a hit.</p><p>Cardoso will need to take a huge leap this season to brighten it up again.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: Trump pulls back on Iran threats for 2 weeks, subject to Iran agreeing to terms
entries.summary: <p>President Donald Trump said he’s pulling back on his threats to widen attacks on Iran. </p><p>The president said that includes an array of bridges, power plants and other civilian targets — subject to Iran being ready for a two-week ceasefire and to reopen Strait of Hormuz. Trump’s latest threat over the Iran war hit a new extreme Tuesday when he warned, “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again,” if Iran fails to make a deal that includes reopening the vital Strait of Hormuz. </p><p>The Republican president’s earlier comments were swiftly met with condemnation from Democrats, some Make America Great Again supporters who have since broken with Trump, and the first pope from the United States.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/illinois-governor/2026/04/07/jb-pritzker-donald-trump-iran-war-power-plants-bridges-threat-25th-amendment" target="_blank" >Gov. JB Pritzker, who is widely seen as a potential 2028 presidential contender,</a> was among those who called for Trump's removal from office for his threats to bomb Iran. </p><p>Early Tuesday, the U.S. struck military targets in the Iranian oil hub of Kharg Island, according to a White House official who was not authorized to speak publicly and spoke on condition of anonymity. The attack marked the second time the island was targeted. Israeli warplanes, meanwhile, struck bridges and railways in Iran.</p><p>Iran’s president said Tuesday that 14 million Iranians, including himself, have volunteered to sacrifice their lives in the war. The figure is double the other figures mentioned by state media in the past. Iran is home to 90 million people.</p><p>U.S. journalist Shelly Kittleson, who was kidnapped in Baghdad last week by an Iran-backed Iraqi militia, was released Tuesday. Ahead of her release, the group said its decision came “in appreciation of the patriotic stances” of Iraq’s prime minister, without giving details.</p><p><i>Check back for updates.</i> </p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: CPS students can now access books, tutoring, other library services with just a student ID
entries.summary: <p>Chicago Public Schools students can now access books, tutors and other resources at city libraries with just their student ID number.</p><p>It's the latest effort by CPS and the Chicago Public Library to expand access to library materials through <a class="Link" href="https://www.chipublib.org/the-81-club/" target="_blank" ><u>The 81 Club</u></a>, an initiative that makes it easier for students to get books and other resources at all 81 public library branches.</p><p>All CPS students are automatically enrolled in the 81 Club, which launched in 2022, and they can now use their ID to check out library materials in person or online “with no application, paperwork or additional steps,” <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/mayor/press_room/press_releases/2026/april/81-club-expansion.html" target="_blank" ><u>according to CPS and CPL</u></a>. Students previously had to pick up 81 Club cards in person at a library. Still, some students may want to apply for a traditional library card, as that allows them to check out more than five physical items at a time.</p><p>Officials with the two agencies said this expansion is meant to help young people take advantage of what the library system has to offer, even if they lack stable housing or legal immigration status in the U.S. — barriers that could make it hard to get to the library in person or provide additional paperwork.</p><p>“We all know too well that talent is equally distributed across this large city in every single neighborhood, but opportunity access is not,” Michael Fassnacht, board chair of the Chicago Public Library Foundation, said during a Tuesday news conference at the Austin Branch Library.</p><p>Through the program, students have access to the library’s online databases and digital media and can request one-on-one tutoring services online or in person, among other services.</p><p>Last year, nearly 3,000 students from 390 different CPS schools activated their 81 Club accounts, <a class="Link" href="https://empowereverychicagoan.org/" target="_blank" ><u>according to the Chicago Library Foundation</u></a>, though that represents a small share of the district’s 316,000 students. It was not immediately clear how many CPS students are using the program now.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Damarious Jefferson, a high school senior in CPS, said he used the 81 Club program to work on projects, study and borrow books.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Damarious Jefferson, a senior at Austin College and Career Academy, said he used the program to further his interest in finance, which he wants to study after high school at Northern Illinois University.</p><p>“It helped me with projects, studying, even just reading a book sometimes,” Jefferson said, adding that he enjoyed it so much he encouraged his friends to use it, too. “I told them it's free to anyone and you should use this for studying, research or even your own work.”</p><p>Teachers can also take part in the program. It grants them access to research databases, instructional tools, as well as eBooks and audiobooks that they can use in the classroom.</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Gluten-free raspberry almond cornmeal cake is unfussy, comforting and deeply satisfying
entries.summary: <p>This simple gluten-free cake is a sweet, reliable pick-me-up. It's the kind of cake you'll find yourself reaching for at any hour of the day, equally at home on the breakfast table, alongside an afternoon cup of coffee or tea, or served as a low-key dessert after dinner.</p><p>Almond flour and cornmeal form the base of the cake, giving it a naturally gluten-free structure with just the right balance of tenderness and texture. Fresh lemon zest brightens the batter, adding a subtle citrus aroma that keeps the cake feeling light, and juicy raspberries are scattered throughout for pops of tart sweetness in every slice.</p><p>Medium-grind cornmeal adds a gentle crunch that contrasts nicely with the moist crumb, adding texture to each bite. If you prefer a softer, more delicate bite, then fine-ground cornmeal can be used instead. Either way, the result is a cake that's unfussy, comforting and deeply satisfying.</p><p>This cake is best enjoyed simply — no frosting required — but the recipe includes a simple lemon syrup. A light dusting of powdered sugar is more than enough, though it's just as lovely served plain, warm from the oven or topped with a dollop of whipped cream. It keeps well for several days, making it a perfect make-ahead option for busy mornings or casual entertaining.</p><h3>Raspberry Almond Cornmeal Cake</h3><p>Active time: 20 minutes<br>Total time: 1 hour and 10 minutes<br>Yield: Makes one (8-inch) cake</p><p><b>Ingredients</b></p><p>1 3/4 cups ground almonds<br>1 cup medium grind or finely ground cornmeal (see note)<br>2 teaspoons baking powder<br>1 teaspoon kosher salt<br>Zest of one lemon, plus juice<br>3 large eggs<br>1 cup granulated sugar, plus 2 tablespoons<br>1/2 teaspoon almond extract<br>1/2 teaspoon vanilla extract<br>1/2 cup fruity olive oil<br>6 ounces fresh raspberries</p><p><b>Directions</b></p><p>Heat the oven to 350 degrees (or 325 degrees for a convection oven). Lightly oil an 8-inch springform pan. Line the bottom with parchment.</p><p>Combine the ground almonds, cornmeal, baking powder, salt and lemon zest in a bowl. Whisk to blend.</p><p>Combine the eggs and 1 cup sugar in a separate large bowl or the bowl of an electric mixer. Mix until light and fluffy. Stir in the almond and vanilla extracts, then stir in the oil until combined. Add the dry ingredients.</p><p>Fold half of the raspberries into the batter. Spoon the batter into the prepared pan and smooth the top. Top with half of the remaining raspberries, gently pressing into the batter.</p><p>Bake until golden and a skewer inserted in the center comes out clean, about 50 minutes.</p><p>While the cake bakes, make the syrup. Combine the lemon juice and the 2 tablespoons sugar in a small saucepan over medium heat. Simmer until the sugar dissolves and the mixture is slightly reduced and syrupy, about 2 minutes.</p><p>When the cake is ready, remove from the oven and transfer to a wire rack. Brush the top with some of the syrup. After 10 minutes, remove the sides of the springform pan. Brush the sides of the cake with the remaining syrup. Cool to room temperature. Serve with the remaining raspberries for garnish.</p><p><i>Lynda Balslev is an award-winning writer, cookbook author and recipe developer, and authors the blog TasteFood.</i></p>
entries.author: Lynda Balslev | TasteFood

entries.title: Réplica de la Oficina Oval del Centro Presidencial Obama en Chicago está casi lista
entries.summary: <p>El diseñador de interiores Michael S. Smith había casi terminado con su extensa remodelación de la Oficina Oval de la Casa Blanca en 2010 cuando sonó su teléfono.</p><p>Era la florería de la Casa Blanca. Querían saber qué flores le gustaría al presidente Barack Obama para la mesa de café de mica de su oficina recién redecorada.</p><p>Smith había aportado tonos cálidos, dramáticas cortinas tejidas color guindo, libros raros, obras de arte de Edward Hopper, cerámica nativa estadounidense y mucho más en su cuidadosa renovación de la Oficina Oval.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-9f0000" name="module-9f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Las decoraciones incluso incluían un pequeño modelo operante del telégrafo original de Samuel Morse, tomado prestado de la Oficina de Patentes de Estados Unidos.</p><p>Sin embargo, no había flores para la mesa de café.</p><p>En cambio, Smith le dio al presidente lo que sería una de las características más populares de la Oficina Oval de Obama: un frutero de madera estilo Shaker lleno de manzanas que estaban a disposición de quien quisiera tomarlas.</p><p>"A pesar de que claramente le encantan las flores, la idea de algo más práctico, algo más estadounidense... fue mucho más interesante y reflexivo para él", recordó Smith. "Perderían ‘X’ docenas de manzanas al día porque la gente las tomaría como un recuerdo".</p><p>Échale un vistazo, <a class="Link" href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/sep/01/trump-oval-office-gold-before-after-decor-white-house-makeover" target="_blank" >si te atreves</a>, a la Oficina Oval de hoy, y no sabrías que el cuidadoso rediseño de Smith alguna vez existió.</p><p>Pero la Oficina Oval de Smith volverá a vivir, no en Washington D.C., sino en el lado sur de Chicago como una réplica a escala detallada, ubicada en la torre del museo del Centro Presidencial Obama de $850 millones en el vecindario de Jackson Park. La Oficina Oval está en el cuarto piso del museo.</p><p>Smith lideró el diseño del nuevo espacio. Y prácticamente todo lo que puso en la Oficina Oval de la era de Obama —muebles, libros, alfombra, obras de arte, hasta el más pequeño detalle— será recreado en el nuevo espacio.</p><p>"Los centros y bibliotecas presidenciales, la recreación de la Oficina Oval siempre es una de las cosas más populares", dijo Smith. "Es emocionante. Es emocionante para mí verlo y yo había estado en la [oficina] real. Pero si no has estado en la real, la idea de poder experimentar inmersivamente algo así es algo mágica".</p><p>Los visitantes podrán entrar a la Oficina Oval del centro presidencial, sentarse detrás de una reproducción del Escritorio Resolute y experimentar la habitación como lo hizo Obama y numerosos visitantes, dignatarios y personal de la Casa Blanca en ese entonces.</p><p>El frutero de manzanas también estará allí. Sin embargo, no intentes comerte una: serán de plástico.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f1d6d8" name="image-f1d6d8"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3f17971/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4096x2299+0+216/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdd%2F6e%2F9637ac3b4337b15c3eae87d89a68%2Fbin-laden-obama.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c0ecc39/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4096x2299+0+216/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdd%2F6e%2F9637ac3b4337b15c3eae87d89a68%2Fbin-laden-obama.JPG class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>En esta foto de 2011, el presidente Barack Obama está sentado detrás del Escritorio Resoluto en la Oficina Oval.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pete Souza/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Smith donó sus archivos de rediseño de la Oficina Oval a la Fundación Obama, que proporcionó a los diseñadores de las exhibiciones del museo la información necesaria para una reproducción detallada y precisa.</p><p>Los trabajadores del museo utilizaron fotografías del espacio para averiguar qué era qué. También recibieron ayuda de la Asociación Histórica de la Casa Blanca, un grupo sin fines de lucro fundado en 1961 por la entonces primera dama Jacqueline Kennedy.</p><p>La Oficina del Conservador de la Casa Blanca también colaboró. La agencia conserva e investiga la abundante colección de muebles, arte y objetos decorativos de la Casa Blanca.</p><p>La directora del museo, Louise Bernard, y su equipo de colecciones y exhibiciones están poniendo más de 70 artículos en exhibición.</p><p>El fabricante de Grand Rapids, Michigan, de la alfombra original de 23 por 30 pies de la Oficina Oval de Obama —la que llevaba el sello presidencial y citas de los presidentes Abraham Lincoln, John F. Kennedy y Franklin Delano Roosevelt— creó una reproducción a tamaño completo para el nuevo espacio.</p><p>El Museo Whitney de Arte Americano de la Ciudad de Nueva York prestó a la Casa Blanca de Obama dos pinturas de Edward Hopper de los años 30, "Burly Cobb’s House, South Truro" y "Cobb’s Barns, South Truro", para exhibirlas en la Oficina Oval real.</p><p>Las reproducciones de las obras de Hopper colgarán en la Oficina Oval en réplica bajo un acuerdo con el Whitney.</p><p>El museo tenía dos copias autorizadas de las pinturas que fueron hechas para una película. "El Whitney siempre pide que cualquier reproducción sea ligeramente más grande o más pequeña que la original", dijo un funcionario del museo del Centro Obama.</p><p>Los artistas nativos estadounidenses de Canadá y Santa Fe, Nuevo México, que crearon la cerámica que estuvo en exhibición en la Oficina Oval de la Casa Blanca de Obama, fueron nuevamente convocados por el equipo de Bernard.</p><p>Una reproducción a tamaño completo del Escritorio Resolute —el gran escritorio de socios tallado a mano que fue elaborado con las vigas de roble del barco británico HMS Resolute y regalado a los Estados Unidos por la Reina Victoria en 1880— está allí. Una empresa en Indonesia fabricó la réplica, dijo Smith.</p><p>El escritorio incluso tiene una réplica del famoso botón de llamada rojo. En la Casa Blanca, el dispositivo llama a un asistente presidencial. No tendrá función en la Oficina Oval del Centro Obama.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-da559b" name="image-da559b"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3acb64f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/654x367+0+34/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F78%2F94168cd142799cdc7d00019be759%2Fimg-9415.jpeg data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d5f11d3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/654x367+0+34/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F78%2F94168cd142799cdc7d00019be759%2Fimg-9415.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Lillian McNally, de veintiún meses, toma una manzana mientras el entonces presidente Barack Obama se reúne con su madre, Amanda Anderson, y a su padre, Matthew McNally, en la Oficina Oval el 8 de octubre de 2015." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d5f11d3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/654x367+0+34/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F78%2F94168cd142799cdc7d00019be759%2Fimg-9415.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/18613c5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/654x367+0+34/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F78%2F94168cd142799cdc7d00019be759%2Fimg-9415.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d5f11d3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/654x367+0+34/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe6%2F78%2F94168cd142799cdc7d00019be759%2Fimg-9415.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Lillian McNally, de veintiún meses, toma una manzana mientras el entonces presidente Barack Obama se reúne con su madre, Amanda Anderson, y a su padre, Matthew McNally, en la Oficina Oval el 8 de octubre de 2015.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>The White House</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Obama participó en la conformación de la nueva Oficina Oval, así como lo ha hecho con todo el campus.</p><p>"El presidente Obama ha desempeñado un papel tan central en el diseño del museo, en términos de la apariencia y la sensación de los espacios, el desarrollo de contenido", dijo Bernard. "Es un lector, escritor, pensador. Es un creativo. Se preocupa profundamente por las artes y la cultura. Es un productor de cine. Tiene un ojo increíblemente exigente".</p><p>Y memoria, aparentemente. Recuerda casi <i>todo</i> sobre la oficina.</p><p>"El mandato que vino del presidente fue asegurarse de que fuera una réplica exacta", dijo Valerie Jarrett, director ejecutivo de la Fundación Obama.</p><p>Durante una reciente visita a la réplica de la Oficina Oval, Obama vio una reproducción del programa de la marcha de Washington de 1963 en un marco. El original —un regalo de un amigo— fue exhibido en la Oficina Oval durante su presidencia.</p><p>"Y señaló que no tenía algunas de las manchas de agua que tenía el programa original", dijo Jarrett. "Pero, aparte de eso, es una réplica absoluta".</p><p>El equipo del museo está trabajando para reproducir la misma iluminación artificial y natural de la Oficina Oval de la Casa Blanca.</p><p>Pero la Oficina Oval del Centro Obama no tiene ventanas exteriores, por lo que los diseñadores utilizarán cajas de luz para imitar la luz solar.</p><p>Jarrett, una asesora presidencial principal en la administración de Obama, pasó incontables horas en la verdadera Oficina Oval.</p><p>"¿Cuál es ese comercial donde dijeron, '¿Es en vivo o es Memorex?'" dijo Jarrett. "Me atrevería a adivinar que las personas que visiten no podrán decir si es en vivo o en Memorex. El arte, la alfombra, los muebles, las manzanas, cada último detalle, creo que a la gente le costaría trabajo diferenciar".</p><p>El Centro Obama ha recibido críticas justificadas desde el punto de vista del diseño, particularmente por la severidad visual y la altura de la torre del museo.</p><p>Por eso, ver lo que habrá dentro de los edificios del campus —y examinar cómo se verán y funcionarán los espacios— es importante.</p><p>"Creo que esto es algo que puede ser muy diferente a otros centros y bibliotecas presidenciales, ya que este edificio está tan orientado a ser una parte activa de la comunidad [local]... pero también una parte de la comunidad internacional", dijo Smith. "Es algo vivo que va a ser sobre mucho más que sólo los ocho años”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Lee Bey

entries.title: Pope says Trump’s threat to destroy Iranian civilization is ‘truly unacceptable’
entries.summary: <p>ROME (AP) — Pope Leo XIV said Tuesday that U.S. President Donald Trump’s threat to <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/middle-east" target="_blank" >destroy Iranian civilization</a> was “truly unacceptable” and said any attacks on civilian infrastructure violate international law.</p><p>In some of his strongest comments yet against the war, the American pope urged Americans and other people of good will to contact their political leaders and congressional representatives to demand they reject war and work for peace.</p><p>“Today as we all know there was this threat against all the people of Iran. This is truly unacceptable,” Leo said as he left his country house in Castel Gandolfo, south of Rome.</p><p>He was referring to Trump’s threat that a “whole civilization will die tonight” if Iran fails to meet his <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/live/iran-war-israel-trump-04-07-2026" target="_blank" >latest deadline</a> to strike a deal that includes reopening <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/strait-of-hormuz" target="_blank" >the Strait of Hormuz</a>.</p><p>Leo recalled his Easter appeal for peace and to reject war, “especially a war which many people have said is an unjust war, which is continuing to escalate, and which is not resolving anything.”</p><p>He invited all people of good will to contact their political leaders and congressional representatives to remind them that attacks on civilian infrastructure are “against international law” and also are a “sign of the hatred, the division, the destruction human beings are capable of, and we all want to work for peace.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/pope-vatican-mideast-peace-dea8fc1412a34e017dfeab1156549b3e" target="_blank" >In recent weeks</a>, history’s first U.S.-born pope has escalated the tone of his opposition to the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran after initially issuing muted appeals for peace and dialogue.</p><p>Last week for the first time, Leo publicly named Trump in saying he hoped the U.S. president was truly “looking for an off-ramp.”</p><p>The Vatican has a tradition of diplomatic neutrality and it is rare for a pope to name a political leader or country specifically in a critical way. But the war in Iran has nudged even a reserved pope to break with typical protocol.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e80000" name="image-e80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
