alt="VATICAN-RELIGION-POPE-REGINA COELI" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c6c0246/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8256x4633+0+435/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F62%2Fa1e972d748a79dce7d898dce50e3%2F2269457224.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fa04521/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8256x4633+0+435/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F62%2Fa1e972d748a79dce7d898dce50e3%2F2269457224.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c6c0246/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8256x4633+0+435/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F62%2Fa1e972d748a79dce7d898dce50e3%2F2269457224.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A man holds the Iranian prerevolution lion and sun flag as Pope Leo XIV addresses the crowd from the window of the apostolic palace overlooking St. Peter’s square during the Regina Coeli prayer in The Vatican on April 6, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>ALBERTO PIZZOLI/Getty</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>On Tuesday, Leo didn’t cite Trump by name but in comments in English he urged people to contact their political leaders and congressional representatives “to ask them, tell them to work for peace and to reject war.”</p><p>“We have a worldwide economic crisis, an energy crisis, (a) situation in the Middle East of great instability, which is only provoking more hatred throughout the world,” he said.</p><p>He said the message to political leaders should be: “Come back to the table, let’s talk, let’s look for solutions in a peaceful way and let’s remember especially the innocent children, the elderly, sick, so many people who have already become or will become victims of this continued warfare.”</p><p>The Vatican is <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/lebanon-christians-easter-hezbollah-israel-war-936e2d8636610149ef1700f6fccdd7c4" target="_blank" >particularly concerned</a> about how the Iran conflict has spread to a renewed war in Lebanon between Israel and the Iranian-backed Lebanese militant Hezbollah group. The Vatican fears for Christians in southern Lebanon, who are an important bulwark for the church in the region.</p><p>Earlier in the day, the Vatican released a special message from Leo to the residents of Debel, Lebanon after a convoy carrying over 40 tons of aid led by the Vatican was prevented from arriving with an Easter shipment. It was canceled for what Lebanon’s Maronite Church said were “security reasons.”</p><p>In the message, Leo expressed solidarity with the “injustices” that the Christians of southern Lebanon are enduring and compared them to Christ’s suffering.</p><p>“In your misfortune, in the injustice you endure, in the feeling of abandonment you experience, you are very close to Jesus. You are close to Him also on this Easter Day when He conquered the forces of evil, and which resonates for you as a promise of the future,” read the message, which was written in French and was signed by the Vatican secretary of state, Cardinal Pietro Parolin.</p><p>Leo visited Lebanon late last year on his first international trip as pope.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Apollo astronaut Jim Lovell left a message that Artemis II crew heard during record moon mission
entries.summary: <p>Before etching their names in spaceflight history, the four Artemis II astronauts got some posthumous words of encouragement from Jim Lovell, one of NASA's greats.</p><p>The longtime resident of Lake Forest, where for years he operated the restaurant Lovell’s of Lake Forest, flew on two Apollo-era missions, in 1968 and 1970. He died last year. But he left behind a message that he recorded for the Artemis crew, which they heard before they became the first humans to fly near the moon in more than 50 years.</p><p>On Monday, astronauts Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch and Victor Glover of NASA and Jeremy Hansen of the Canadian Space Agency flew by the moon in a mission similar to the Apollo 8 flyby of which Lovell was a part.</p><p>And, in a bit of serendipity, it wasn't long before a nearly seven-hour period of lunar observations began that the Artemis II crew broke one of Lovell's own records: surpassing the distance from Earth that the ill-fated Apollo 13 mission reached under Lovell's command.</p><p>Artemis II is the first human spaceflight under NASA's Artemis program, the main objective of which is to return humanity to the surface of the moon for the first time since the Apollo 17 mission in 1972.</p><p>But the Artemis campaign is far from a reprise of the Apollo era. This time, NASA is aiming for humans not to simply leave but to remain to set up a moon base toward the lunar south pole ahead of what are planned to be the first crewed expeditions to Mars.</p><p>Lovell, a Cleveland native, was selected for two moon missions during NASA's Apollo era.</p><p>The first was Apollo 8, which sent Lovell, Frank Borman and Williams Anders on a six-day trip orbiting the moon in 1968. As with Artemis II, the mission served as an important prelude to a moon landing, helping test systems, hardware and procedures before astronauts stepped foot on the surface during Apollo 11 in 1969.</p><p>Lovell also was the commander of the nearly disastrous Apollo 13 mission, which in 1970 was meant to be the third human moon landing.</p><p>But an oxygen tank that exploded on the service module transformed the spaceflight into a life-or-death situation recounted in the hit 1995 movie “Apollo 13,” in which Tom Hanks played Lovell. Relying on backup life-support systems on the Apollo Lunar Module meant to transport them to the moon's surface from orbit, the astronauts used primitive methods of celestial navigation to make their way back to Earth.</p><p>Lovell was 97 when he died last Aug. 7, so he wasn’t around to witness humanity's return to the moon's vicinity with the April 1 launch of Artemis II from the Kennedy Space Center in Florida. But he recorded a message in advance for the four astronauts selected for the mission that they were able to hear Monday.</p><p>"Welcome to my old neighborhood," Lovell said in the message<a class="Link" href="https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-crew-ready-for-lunar-flyby/" target="_blank" >shared by NASA</a>, which was played aboard NASA's Orion crew capsule as the astronauts prepared for a historic lunar flyby.</p><p>Referring to his Apollo 8 mission, Lovell said he and his crew were treated to "a view of the home planet that inspired and united people around the world."</p><p>"I’m proud to pass that torch on to you," Lovell told the Artemis II astronauts. "It’s a historic day, and I know how busy you’ll be. But don’t forget to enjoy the view."</p><p>Lovell concluded: "Good luck and Godspeed from all of us here on the good Earth.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ce0000" name="image-ce0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4977e73/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6c%2F69%2Fe77eae0444a98e22b8b77f2451e4%2F2269703202.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d5c545a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6c%2F69%2Fe77eae0444a98e22b8b77f2451e4%2F2269703202.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/df8891a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6c%2F69%2Fe77eae0444a98e22b8b77f2451e4%2F2269703202.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Artemis II crew captured this image of a crescent Earth setting on the Moon’s limb on Monday as they came close to passing behind the Moon. In this photo, provided by NASA, the dark portion of Earth is experiencing nighttime, while Australia and Oceania are in daylight. In the foreground, the Ohm crater is visible, with terraced edges and a flat floor interrupted by central peaks. Peaks such as these form in complex craters when the lunar surface is liquified on impact, and the liquefied surface splashes upward during the crater’s formation. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/df8891a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6c%2F69%2Fe77eae0444a98e22b8b77f2451e4%2F2269703202.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0acf522/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6c%2F69%2Fe77eae0444a98e22b8b77f2451e4%2F2269703202.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/df8891a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6c%2F69%2Fe77eae0444a98e22b8b77f2451e4%2F2269703202.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Artemis II crew captured this image of a crescent Earth setting on the Moon’s limb on Monday as they came close to passing behind the Moon. In this photo, provided by NASA, the dark portion of Earth is experiencing nighttime, while Australia and Oceania are in daylight. In the foreground, the Ohm crater is visible, with terraced edges and a flat floor interrupted by central peaks. Peaks such as these form in complex craters when the lunar surface is liquified on impact, and the liquefied surface splashes upward during the crater’s formation. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>NASA via Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Artemis II crew got the message while on the cusp of making spaceflight history during their lunar flyby. Before a nearly seven-hour observation period even began, the Artemis II astronauts already had traveled farther from Earth than any humans before them, surpassing a record set in 1970 during Lovell's ill-fated Apollo 13 mission. That mission, which required an emergency return, reached a distance of 248,655 miles from Earth.</p><p>The Artemis II astronauts passed that milestone, then went thousands of miles farther, reaching a maximum distance from Earth of 252,756 miles, setting a record for human spaceflight, according to NASA.</p><p>Swooping around the moon's far side, the Orion vehicle carrying the Artemis II astronauts came no closer than 4,067 miles above the lunar surface during the historic rendezvous. That altitude allowed the crew to see the full disk of our celestial neighbor as the astronauts witnessed lunar sights that no other humans before — not even the Apollo crew — have experienced in person.</p><p>The Orion capsule is due to make a water landing Friday in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of San Diego, after which five orange airbags will inflate around the top of the spacecraft and flip the capsule into an upright position. After the landing, the plan is for the crew to exit the vehicle onto a Navy recovery vessel within about two hours.</p><p><i>To read more from USA Today, </i><a class="Link" href="https://www.usatoday.com/" target="_blank" ><i>click here</i></a><i>. </i></p>
entries.author: Eric Lagatta | USA Today

entries.title: Cubs right-hander Cade Horton to undergo season-ending elbow surgery
entries.summary: <p>ST. PETERSBURG, Fla. — The Cubs entered this season with the depth to withstand pitching injuries, but there’s no replacing Cade Horton.</p><p>Manager Craig Counsell announced that Horton, one of the game’s best young pitchers, will miss the rest of the season and undergo season-ending elbow surgery after imaging revealed damage to the UCL.</p><p>Horton left Friday’s start against the Guardians in the second inning, complaining of wrist discomfort that progressed to his forearm. He returned to Chicago on Monday for more imaging and tests.</p><p>Horton posted a 2.67 ERA across 22 starts and earned nine first-place votes in Rookie of the Year voting. Horton raised the level of his performance in the second half, posting a 1.03 ERA and holding opposing batters to a .154/.213/.234 slash line .</p><p>His play was so impactful that Counsell pushed back on the notion of another next step for Horton because of how good he was.</p><p>After an impressive first start of the 2026 season, Horton looked to be on the cusp of stardom, now he faces a career pause as he rehabilitates his throwing arm.</p><p>“There are things that get thrown in front of you that are big,” Counsell said, “and you have to process it on a day like today for Cade. There are a lot of long days of monotonous work ahead of you.”</p><p>The specific injury Horton will undergo has yet to be determined. Horton, the No. 7 pick in the 2022 MLB Draft, already had Tommy John surgery in his young career while at Oklahoma in 2021. Knowing the delicate nature of young starting pitchers and how susceptible they are to injuries, the Cubs limited Horton’s innings last season to avoid this situation.</p><p>Pitching injuries remain unpredictable, no matter how cautious an organization is.</p><p>“Anytime a guy goes on the mound, you do everything you possibly can to learn from the things that don't go well,” general manager Carter Hawkins said. “It's easy to throw your hands up and go, ‘Hey, it just happens.’ I think that's kind of a lazy one to do. I think even [in] a case like this, where we can't pinpoint what went wrong or what we might have done wrong, [you still] try to figure out what potentially we could do better moving forward and apply those learnings and then try to get better.</p><p>"We've been doing that for years now, and I think we're limiting the risk of our pitchers because of that, [but] it doesn't eliminate the risk.”</p><p>Barring a trade, the Cubs will lack a pitcher of Horton’s caliber. His injury sets off several pitching changes: Colin Rea will likely move into the rotation, requiring the bullpen to adjust and cover his innings.</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9cfa476/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6449x4317+13+0/resize/490x328!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8b%2Fcb%2F153961554c14a571b88ea718e354%2Fcubs-guardians-baseball.jpg"> </picture> </a></div> <div class="PagePromoB-content"> <div class="PagePromo-category"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> </div> <div class="PagePromo-title"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/05/cubs-split-doubleheader-drop-series-to-guardians" >Cubs split doubleheader, drop series to Guardians</a> </div> <div class="PagePromo-description">The Cubs won Game 1 with just two hits. It was the first time they’ve won a game with two or fewer hits since August 2022 against the Brewers. </div> <div class="PagePromo-byline"><div class="PagePromo-date"> <bsp-timestamp data-timestamp=1775446522000 data-promo="true"> <template data-date-tpl>[month] [day], [year], [hour]:[minute][ampm] [timezone]</template> <span data-date> </span> </bsp-timestamp><span class="dot"></span></div> <div class="PagePromo-author"> <span>By </span><strong><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/kyle-williams" >Kyle Williams </a></strong> </div> <div class="PagePromo-readingTime"> <span class="dot-divider"></span> <svg class="PagePromo-readingTime-icon"> <use xlink:href="#icon-clock-reading"></use> </svg> <span class="PagePromo-readingTime-text"> <bsp-time-to-read data-article-word-count="544"></bsp-time-to-read> read </span> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </bsp-listcarousel><p>Left-hander Justin Steele, the 2024 Opening Day starter, is progressing in his return from UCL revision surgery that ended his 2025 season with eyes on a May or June return. But at the beginning of the season, Steele’s return was considered a bonus. Now, it might be necessary for him to return to the mound in top form.</p><p>The Cubs’ pitching depth helps with short-term injuries, but losing Horton for the season is a fatal blow. Javier Assad and Rea might raise the floor, but Horton raised the ceiling.</p><p>Horton’s presence gave the Cubs belief they could go deep in the playoffs. Even in just his second season, he proved irreplaceable.</p><p>“But it's not all on Colin, or it's not all on the guy that replaces Colin in the bullpen,” Counsell said. “We all have to just do our part.”<br></p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: 'Broadview 6' judge moves on from White House interference claims after feds' denial
entries.summary: <p>The federal judge presiding over the conspiracy case against the remaining members of the “Broadview Six” largely closed the book Tuesday<b> </b>on a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >bid for White House communications</a> in the case, after prosecutors denied<b> </b>that any such correspondence exists.</p><p>The feds say “there are no communications of any nature from, to, or with anyone” outside the U.S. attorney’s office, aside from local FBI investigators, and they’ve made clear that “includes anyone in the White House and all components and offices of Main Justice.”</p><p>That’s despite the fact that at least one defendant in the case, former congressional candidate Kat Abughazaleh, had the attention of White House Deputy Press Secretary Abigail Jackson and far-right influencer Laura Loomer.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" >Remaining Broadview 6 defendants want records showing Trump administration’s influence on conspiracy case</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Defense attorneys wanted U.S. District Judge April Perry to order prosecutors<b> </b>to explain how they reached their conclusion, but she declined to do so Tuesday<b> </b>in the latest hearing in the case. She said the feds know how to do their job and that “their law licenses … are on the line.”</p><p>The judge ultimately denied as moot the defense bid for such communications, meaning the issue had already been resolved by the feds’ claims. She also denied other defense requests related to selective prosecution claims, noting that “being a politician is not a protected class.”</p><p>Being a member of a certain political party could qualify, but she told defense attorneys “you haven’t shown me any evidence that that was considered here.”</p><p>The defense recently disclosed a note<b> </b>sent by the federal agent at the center of the case, in which he identified the political positions held by some of the defendants. But, Perry noted, it did not mention their parties.</p><p>Besides, the agent also described one defendant as being an “Oak Park/Oakbrook Board of Trustees Member.”</p><p>“There’s a big difference between an Oak Park politician and an Oak Brook politician,” Perry remarked, prompting smiles among the defendants.</p><p>Point being, it was hard to make inferences from the note.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/30/broadview-six-ice-trump-doj-justice-chicago-midway-blitz" >Selective prosecution claims in ‘Broadview Six’ case the result of ‘fevered paranoia,’ feds say</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Charged in the case are four people involved in local Democratic politics. They are Abughazaleh, Oak Park village trustee Brian Straw, 45th Ward Democratic committeeperson Michael Rabbitt and Andre Martin, a member of Abughazaleh’s campaign staff. The feds <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" target="_blank" >also dropped charges</a><b> </b>against two others originally charged in the case.</p><p>The group is accused of a conspiracy<b> </b>to impede a federal agent. The case revolves around events on the morning of Sept. 26, outside the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in Broadview during protests against the Operation Midway Blitz deportation campaign.</p><p>The defendants are accused of being part of a group that surrounded the agent’s vehicle<b> </b>and pushed, scratched and otherwise damaged it.</p><p>The case is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/28/broadview-six-conspiracy-case-involving-abughazaleh-could-go-to-trial-in-spring" target="_blank" >on track for trial</a> May 26, though a motion to dismiss the conspiracy charge on First Amendment grounds is still pending. Each defendant also faces a separate misdemeanor charge.</p><p>Defense attorneys said it’s another example of President Donald Trump’s administration using the Justice Department to target his “perceived political enemies.”<b> </b>Prosecutors said any claim of selective or vindictive prosecution is “the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/30/broadview-six-ice-trump-doj-justice-chicago-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >product of fevered paranoia</a> and delusional speculation.”</p><p>The note Perry referenced Tuesday was sent by the agent to three senior ICE officials the day the original six defendants were indicted, defense attorneys have said.</p><p>“Among the 6 defendants,” he wrote, “there is a US congressional candidate, a 45th ward Committee Person, and a Oak Park/Oakbrook Board of Trustees Member.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Text messages allegedly sent by the agent at the center of the “Broadview Six” conspiracy case.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. District Court records</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In a separate text exchange the day the indictment was unsealed, someone told the agent, “You’re going to be famous bruh.”</p><p>The agent allegedly wrote back, “Only among lefties.”</p><p>But Perry wasn’t convinced Tuesday.</p><p>“Public officials being charged is the bread and butter of the U.S. attorney’s office,” she said, noting that it is “always considered a plus factor.”</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel
